subject: Mathematics
question_text: Consider the following information regarding the number of men and women workers in three factories I, II and III Men workers Women workers I 30 25 II 25 31 III 27 26 Represent the above information in the form of a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix. What does the entry in the third row and second column represent?
question_html:
Consider the following information regarding the number of men and women workers in three factories I, II and III

  Men workers Women workers
I 30 25
II 25 31
III 27 26

Represent the above information in the form of a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix. What does the entry in the third row and second column represent?
solution_text: The information is represented in the form of a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix as follows {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {30} & {25} \\ {25} & {31} \\ {27} & {26} \end{array}\right]{/tex} The entry in the third row and the second column represents the number of women workers in factory III.
solution_html:
The information is represented in the form of a 3{tex}\times{/tex}2 matrix as follows
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {30} & {25} \\ {25} & {31} \\ {27} & {26} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
The entry in the third row and the second column represents the number of women workers in factory III.

question_html: If a matrix has 8 elements, what are the possible orders it can have?

solution_html: {tex}1 \times 8,8 \times 1,4 \times 2,2 \times 4{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Construct a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix whose elements are given by {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{1}{2}|i-3 j|{/tex}
question_html: Construct a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix whose elements are given by {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{1}{2}|i-3 j|{/tex}
solution_text: In general a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is given by {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {a_{11}} & {a_{12}} \\ {a_{21}} & {a_{22}} \\ {a_{31}} & {a_{32}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |i - 3 j|, i = 1, 2, 3 and j = 1, 2. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a_{11}=\frac{1}{2}|1-3 \times 1|=1{/tex} , {tex}a_{12}=\frac{1}{2}|1-3 \times 2|=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{21}=\frac{1}{2}|2-3 \times 1|=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , {tex}a_{22}=\frac{1}{2}|2-3 \times 2|=2{/tex} {tex}a_{31}=\frac{1}{2}|3-3 \times 1|=0{/tex} , {tex}a_{32}=\frac{1}{2}|3-3 \times 2|=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Hence the required matrix is given by A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & \frac{5}{2} \\ \frac{1}{2} & {2}\\ {0} & {\frac{3}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
In general a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is given by {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {a_{11}} & {a_{12}} \\ {a_{21}} & {a_{22}} \\ {a_{31}} & {a_{32}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} |i - 3 j|, i = 1, 2, 3 and j = 1, 2.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}     {tex}a_{11}=\frac{1}{2}|1-3 \times 1|=1{/tex}, {tex}a_{12}=\frac{1}{2}|1-3 \times 2|=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a_{21}=\frac{1}{2}|2-3 \times 1|=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}a_{22}=\frac{1}{2}|2-3 \times 2|=2{/tex}
{tex}a_{31}=\frac{1}{2}|3-3 \times 1|=0{/tex}, {tex}a_{32}=\frac{1}{2}|3-3 \times 2|=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Hence the required matrix is given by A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & \frac{5}{2} \\ \frac{1}{2} & {2}\\ {0} & {\frac{3}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{x+3} & {z+4} & {2 y-7} \\ {-6} & {a-1} & {0} \\ {b-3} & {-21} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{0} & {6} & {3 y-2} \\ {-6} & {-3} & {2 c+2} \\ {2 b+4} & {-21} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , Find the values of a, b, c, x, y and z.
question_html: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{x+3} & {z+4} & {2 y-7} \\ {-6} & {a-1} & {0} \\ {b-3} & {-21} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{0} & {6} & {3 y-2} \\ {-6} & {-3} & {2 c+2} \\ {2 b+4} & {-21} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}, Find the values of a, b, c, x, y and z.
solution_text: As the given matrices are equal, therefore, their corresponding elements must be equal. Comparing the corresponding elements, we get x + 3 = 0, z + 4 = 6, 2y – 7 = 3y –2 a – 1 = –3, 0 = 2c + 2 b – 3 = 2b + 4 Simplifying, we get a = – 2, b = –7, c = –1, x = –3, y = –5, z = 2
solution_html:
As the given matrices are equal, therefore, their corresponding elements must be equal. Comparing the corresponding elements, we get
x + 3 = 0,
z + 4 = 6,
2y – 7 = 3y –2
a – 1 = –3,
0 = 2c + 2
b – 3 = 2b + 4
Simplifying, we get a = – 2, b = –7, c = –1, x = –3, y = –5, z = 2

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value of a, b, c and d from the following equation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2 a+b} & {a-2 b} \\ {5 c-d} & {4 c+3 d}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-3} \\ {11} & {24}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the value of a, b, c and d from the following equation:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2 a+b} & {a-2 b} \\ {5 c-d} & {4 c+3 d}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-3} \\ {11} & {24}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2 a+b} & {a-2 b} \\ {5 c-d} & {4 c+3 d}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-3} \\ {11} & {24}\end{array}\right]{/tex} As the given matrices are equal, therefore their corresponding elements must be equal. Comparing the corresponding elements, we get 2a + b = 4 ...(i) a - 2b = -3 .....(ii) 5c - d = 11 ....(iii) 4c + 3d = 24 ....(iv) Multiplying (i) by 2 and adding to (ii), we get 5a = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 4 - 2(1) = 2 Multiplying (iii) by 3 and adding to (iv), we get 19c = 57 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 5(3) - 11 = 4 Hence, a = 1, b = 2, c = 3, d = 4
solution_html:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2 a+b} & {a-2 b} \\ {5 c-d} & {4 c+3 d}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-3} \\ {11} & {24}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
As the given matrices are equal, therefore their corresponding elements must be equal.
Comparing the corresponding elements, we get
2a + b = 4 ...(i)
a - 2b = -3 .....(ii)
5c - d = 11 ....(iii)
4c + 3d = 24 ....(iv)
Multiplying (i) by 2 and adding to (ii), we get
5a = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 4 - 2(1) = 2
Multiplying (iii) by 3 and adding to (iv), we get
19c = 57 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 5(3) - 11 = 4
Hence, a = 1, b = 2, c = 3, d = 4

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Given A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {\sqrt{3}} & {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {\sqrt{5}} & {1} \\ {-2} & {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} find A + B
question_html: Given A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {\sqrt{3}} & {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {\sqrt{5}} & {1} \\ {-2} & {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} find A + B
solution_text: Since A, B are of the same order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3. Therefore, the addition of A and B is defined and is given by A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2+\sqrt{3}} & {1+\sqrt{5}} & {1-1} \\ {2-2} & {3+3} & {0+\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2+\sqrt{3}} & {1+\sqrt{5}} & {0} \\ {0} & {6} & {\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Since A, B are of the same order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3. Therefore, the addition of A and B is defined and is given by
A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2+\sqrt{3}} & {1+\sqrt{5}} & {1-1} \\ {2-2} & {3+3} & {0+\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2+\sqrt{3}} & {1+\sqrt{5}} & {0} \\ {0} & {6} & {\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {2} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-1} & {0} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then find 2A – B.
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {2} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-1} & {0} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then find 2A – B.
solution_text: We have 2A – B = 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {2} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-1} & {0} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {4} & {6} \\ {4} & {6} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-3} & {1} & {-3} \\ {1} & {0} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2-3} & {4+1} & {6-3} \\ {4+1} & {6+0} & {2-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {3} \\ {5} & {6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
2A – B = 2{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {2} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-1} & {0} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {4} & {6} \\ {4} & {6} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-3} & {1} & {-3} \\ {1} & {0} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2-3} & {4+1} & {6-3} \\ {4+1} & {6+0} & {2-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {3} \\ {5} & {6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ 3 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ { - 2} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 4 \\ { - 5} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then find the matrix X such that 2A + 3X = 5B.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ 3 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ { - 2} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 4 \\ { - 5} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find the matrix X such that 2A + 3X = 5B.
solution_text: Given:3X = 5B - 2A {tex}=5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 4 \\ { - 5} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] - 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ 3 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ { - 2} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ {20} \\ { - 25} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 10} \\ {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 16} \\ { - 8} \\ { - 6} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ 4 \\ { - 12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 6} \\ {12} \\ { - 31} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 10} \\ {14} \\ { - 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}X = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 6} \\ {12} \\ { - 31} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 10} \\ {14} \\ { - 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given:3X = 5B - 2A
{tex}=5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 4 \\ { - 5} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] - 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8 \\ 4 \\ 3 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ { - 2} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ {20} \\ { - 25} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 10} \\ {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 16} \\ { - 8} \\ { - 6} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ 4 \\ { - 12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 6} \\ {12} \\ { - 31} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 10} \\ {14} \\ { - 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}X = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 6} \\ {12} \\ { - 31} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 10} \\ {14} \\ { - 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find x and y if x + y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and x - y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&6 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find x and y if x + y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and x - y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&6 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: x + y + x - y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&6 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}2x = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&8 \\ 0&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}x = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&4 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And, (x + y) - (x - y) {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&6 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} x + y - x + y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 4} \\ 0&{10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 0&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
x + y + x - y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&6 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}2x = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&8 \\ 0&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}x = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&4 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And, (x + y) - (x - y) {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 0&9 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&6 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
x + y - x + y {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 4} \\ 0&{10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 0&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of x and y from the following equation: 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the values of x and y from the following equation: 2{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x} & {10} \\ {14} & {2 y-6}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {10 - 4} \\ {14 + 1} & {2 y-6 + 2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {6} \\ {15} & {2 y-4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} or 2x + 3 = 7 and 2y - 4 = 14 (why?) or 2x = 7 - 3 and 2y = 18 or x = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac{18}{2}{/tex} i.e. x = 2 and y = 9
solution_html:
We have,
2{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x} & {10} \\ {14} & {2 y-6}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {-4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {10 - 4} \\ {14 + 1} & {2 y-6 + 2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {6} \\ {15} & {2 y-4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {6} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
or 2x + 3 = 7 and 2y - 4 = 14 (why?)
or 2x = 7 - 3 and 2y = 18
or x = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac{18}{2}{/tex}
i.e. x = 2 and y = 9

If both farmers receive 2% profit on gross sales, compute the profit for each farmer and for each variety sold in October.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find AB, if {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {9} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {6} & {0} \\ {7} & {9} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find AB, if {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {9} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {6} & {0} \\ {7} & {9} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Matrix A has 2 columns which is equal to the number of rows of B. Hence AB is defined. Now {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {6(2)+9(7)} & {6(6)+9(9)} & {6(0)+9(8)} \\ {2(2)+3(7)} & {2(6)+3(9)} & {2(0)+3(8)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {12+63} & {36+81} & {0+72} \\ {4+21} & {12+27} & {0+24} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {75} & {117} & {72} \\ {25} & {39} & {24} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Matrix A has 2 columns which is equal to the number of rows of B. Hence AB is defined. Now

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-4} & {2} & {5} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then find AB, BA. Show that AB {tex}\neq{/tex} BA
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-4} & {2} & {5} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then find AB, BA. Show that AB {tex}\neq{/tex} BA
solution_text: Since A is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix and B is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix. Hence AB and BA are both defined and are matrices of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 and 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, respectively. Now, {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-4} & {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2-8+6} & {3-10+3} \\ {-8+8+10} & {-12+10+5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {0} & {-4} \\ {10} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}B A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-4} & {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2-12} & {-4+6} & {6+15} \\ {4-20} & {-8+10} & {12+25} \\ {2-4} & {-4+2} & {6+5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-10} & {2} & {21} \\ {-16} & {2} & {37} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {11} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Clearly AB {tex}\neq{/tex} BA In the above example, both AB and BA are of different orders and so AB {tex}\neq{/tex} BA.
solution_html:
Since A is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix and B is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix.
Hence AB and BA are both defined and are matrices of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 and 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, respectively.
Now,{tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-4} & {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2-8+6} & {3-10+3} \\ {-8+8+10} & {-12+10+5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {0} & {-4} \\ {10} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}B A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-2} & {3} \\ {-4} & {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2-12} & {-4+6} & {6+15} \\ {4-20} & {-8+10} & {12+25} \\ {2-4} & {-4+2} & {6+5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-10} & {2} & {21} \\ {-16} & {2} & {37} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {11} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Clearly AB {tex}\neq{/tex} BA
In the above example, both AB and BA are of different orders and so AB {tex}\neq{/tex} BA.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find AB, if A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {5} \\ {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find AB, if A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {5} \\ {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {2} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {5} \\ {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Thus, if the product of two matrices is a zero matrix, it is not necessary that one of the matrices is a zero matrix.
solution_html:
We have {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {2} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {5} \\ {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Thus, if the product of two matrices is a zero matrix, it is not necessary that one of the matrices is a zero matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {0} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {0} & {2} \\ {-1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1} & {2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {2} & {0} & {-2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} find A(BC), (AB)C and show that (AB)C = A(BC).
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {0} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {0} & {2} \\ {-1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1} & {2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {2} & {0} & {-2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} find A(BC), (AB)C and show that (AB)C = A(BC).
solution_html:
We have {tex}\mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {0} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {3} \\ {0} & {2} \\ {-1} & {4} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+0+1} & {3+2-4} \\ {2+0-3} & {6+0+12} \\ {3+0-2} & {9-2+8} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {18} \\ {1} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(AB) (C) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {18} \\ {1} & {15} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1} & {2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {2} & {0} & {-2} & {1} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {2+2} & {4+0} & {6-2} & {-8+1} \\ {-1+36} & {-2+0} & {-3-36} & {4+18} \\ {1+30} & {2+0} & {3-30} & {-4+15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
              = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {4} & {4} & {4} & {-7} \\ {35} & {-2} & {-39} & {22} \\ {31} & {2} & {-27} & {11} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now,   BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {0} & {2} \\ {-1} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1} & {2} & {3} & {-4} \\ {2} & {0} & {-2} & {1} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1+6} & {2+0} & {3-6} & {-4+3} \\ {0+4} & {0+0} & {0-4} & {0+2} \\ {-1+8} & {-2+0} & {-3-8} & {4+4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
                 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {7} & {2} & {-3} & {-1} \\ {4} & {0} & {-4} & {2} \\ {7} & {-2} & {-11} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, A(BC) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {0} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rrrr} {7} & {2} & {-3} & {-1} \\ {4} & {0} & {-4} & {2} \\ {7} & {-2} & {-11} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
                            = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {7+4-7} & {2+0+2} & {-3-4+11} & {-1+2-8} \\ {14+0+21} & {4+0-6} & {-6+0-33} & {-2+0+24} \\ {21-4+14} & {6+0-4} & {-9+4-22} & {-3-2+16} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
                            = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {4} & {4} & {4} & {-7} \\ {35} & {-2} & {-39} & {22} \\ {31} & {2} & {-27} & {11} \end{array}\right]{/tex},
   Clearly, (AB) C = A (BC)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Calculate AC, BC and (A + B)C. Also, verify that (A + B)C = AC + BC
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}Calculate AC, BC and (A + B)C. Also, verify that (A + B)C = AC + BC
solution_text: We have, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right] +\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So (A + B) C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-14+24} \\ {-10+0+30} \\ {16+12+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(i){/tex} Further AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-12+21} \\ {-12+0+24} \\ {14+16+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0-2+3} \\ {2+0+6} \\ {2-4+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So AC + BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(ii){/tex} Clearly, from (i) and (ii), it follows that (A + B) C = AC + BC
solution_html:
We have, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right] +\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So (A + B) C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-14+24} \\ {-10+0+30} \\ {16+12+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(i){/tex}
Further AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-12+21} \\ {-12+0+24} \\ {14+16+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0-2+3} \\ {2+0+6} \\ {2-4+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So AC + BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(ii){/tex}
Clearly, from (i) and (ii), it follows that
(A + B) C = AC + BC

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \\ 4&2&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then show that A 3 - 23A – 40 I = 0
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \\ 4&2&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then show that A3 - 23A – 40 I = 0
solution_html:
We have A2 = A.A {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \\ 4&2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \\ 4&2&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {19}&4&8 \\ 1&{12}&8 \\ {14}&6&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
So A3 = A.A2 {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \\ 4&2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {19}&4&8 \\ 1&{12}&8 \\ {14}&6&{15} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {63}&{46}&{69} \\ {69}&{ - 6}&{23} \\ {92}&{46}&{63} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now
A3 - 23A - 40I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {63}&{46}&{69} \\ {69}&{ - 6}&{23} \\ {92}&{46}&{63} \end{array}} \right] - 23\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&3 \\ 3&{ - 2}&1 \\ 4&2&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}- 40\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {63}&{46}&{69} \\ {69}&{ - 6}&{23} \\ {92}&{46}&{63} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 23}&{ - 46}&{ - 69} \\ { - 69}&{46}&{ - 23} \\ { - 92}&{ - 46}&{ - 23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 40}&0&0 \\ 0&{ - 40}&0 \\ 0&0&{ - 40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {63 - 23 - 40}&{46 - 46 + 0}&{69 - 69 + 0} \\ {69 - 69 + 0}&{ - 6 + 46 - 40}&{23 - 23 + 0} \\ {92 - 92 + 0}&{46 - 46 + 0}&{63 - 23 - 40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: In a legislative assembly election, a political group hired a public relations firm to promote its candidate in three ways: telephone, house calls, and letters. The cost per contact (in paise) is given in matrix A as The number of contacts of each type made in two cities X and Y is given by Find the total amount spent by the group in the two cities X and Y.
question_html:
In a legislative assembly election, a political group hired a public relations firm to promote its candidate in three ways: telephone, house calls, and letters. The cost per contact (in paise) is given in matrix A as

The number of contacts of each type made in two cities X and Y is given by

Find the total amount spent by the group in the two cities X and Y.
solution_text: We have {tex}{\text{BA}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {40,000 + 50,000 + 250,000} \\ {120,000 + 100,000 + 500,000} \end{array}} \right]\mathop {}\nolimits_{ \to {\text{Y}}}^{ \to {\text{X}}} {/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {340,000} \\ {720,000} \end{array}} \right]\mathop {}\nolimits_{ \to {\text{Y}}}^{ \to {\text{X}}} {/tex} So the total amount spent by the group in the two cities is 340,000 paise and 720,000 paise, i.e., ₹3400 and ₹7200, respectively.
solution_html:
We have
{tex}{\text{BA}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {40,000 + 50,000 + 250,000} \\ {120,000 + 100,000 + 500,000} \end{array}} \right]\mathop {}\nolimits_{ \to {\text{Y}}}^{ \to {\text{X}}} {/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {340,000} \\ {720,000} \end{array}} \right]\mathop {}\nolimits_{ \to {\text{Y}}}^{ \to {\text{X}}} {/tex}
So the total amount spent by the group in the two cities is 340,000 paise and 720,000 paise, i.e., ₹3400 and ₹7200, respectively.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} verify that (A′)′ = A (A + B)′ = A′ + B′ (kB)′ = kB′, where k is any constant.
question_html:
If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} verify that

  1. (A′)′ = A
  2. (A + B)′ = A′ + B′
  3. (kB)′ = kB′, where k is any constant.
solution_text: We have A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {2} & {0} \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A')' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A Thus (A′)′ = A We have A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {4} \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {5} & {\sqrt{3}-1} & {4} \\ {5} & {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore (A + B)' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {5} \\ {\sqrt{3}} {-1} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {5} \\ {\sqrt{3}} {-1} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Thus (A + B)′ = A′ + B′ We have, {tex}kB{/tex} = k {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2 k} & {-k} & {2 k} \\ {k} & {2 k} & {4 k} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(kB)'{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2 k} & {k} \\ {-k} & {2 k} \\ {2 k} & {4 k} \end{array}\right]=k\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]=k \mathrm{B}^{\prime}{/tex} Thus {tex}(kB)' = kB'{/tex}
solution_html:
1. We have
    A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right] {/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {2} & {0} \end{array}\right] {/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (A')' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A
    Thus (A′)′ = A
  2. We have
    A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {4} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {4} \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {5} & {\sqrt{3}-1} & {4} \\ {5} & {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Therefore (A + B)' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {5} \\ {\sqrt{3}} {-1} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Now A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    So A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {5} \\ {\sqrt{3}} {-1} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Thus (A + B)′ = A′ + B′
  3. We have,
    {tex}kB{/tex} = k{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2 k} & {-k} & {2 k} \\ {k} & {2 k} & {4 k} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(kB)'{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2 k} & {k} \\ {-k} & {2 k} \\ {2 k} & {4 k} \end{array}\right]=k\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]=k \mathrm{B}^{\prime}{/tex}
    Thus {tex}(kB)' = kB'{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ; Verify that (AB)' = B'A'.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ; Verify that (AB)' = B'A'.
solution_text: {tex}AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&{ - 6}&{12} \\ 4&{12}&{ - 24} \\ 5&{15}&{ - 30} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left( {AB} \right)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \\ { - 6}&{12}&{15} \\ {12}&{ - 24}&{ - 30} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}B' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 3 \\ { - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}B'A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 3 \\ { - 6} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \\ { - 6}&{12}&{15} \\ {12}&{ - 24}&{ - 30} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Hence (AB)' = B'. A'
solution_html:
{tex}AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&{ - 6}&{12} \\ 4&{12}&{ - 24} \\ 5&{15}&{ - 30} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\left( {AB} \right)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \\ { - 6}&{12}&{15} \\ {12}&{ - 24}&{ - 30} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}B' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 3 \\ { - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}B'A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 3 \\ { - 6} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&4&5 \\ { - 6}&{12}&{15} \\ {12}&{ - 24}&{ - 30} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Hence (AB)' = B'. A'

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Express the matrix {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 2}&{ - 4} \\ { - 1}&3&4 \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix.
question_html: Express the matrix {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 2}&{ - 4} \\ { - 1}&3&4 \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
{tex}B' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&1 \\ { - 2}&3&{ - 2} \\ { - 4}&4&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Let {tex}P = \frac{1}{2}(B + B') = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&3&1 \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&1&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}P' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&3&1 \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&1&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = P{/tex}
Thus {tex}P = \frac{1}{2}(B + B'){/tex} is a symmetric matrix
Let {tex}Q = \frac{1}{3}(B - B') = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 5}}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}}&0&3 \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{ - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}Q' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&{\frac{5}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&0&{ - 3} \\ {\frac{{ - 5}}{2}}&3&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}Q' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 5}}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}}&0&3 \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{ - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Q' = -Q
Thus {tex}Q = \frac{1}{2}(B - B'){/tex} is a skew symmetric matrix
{tex}P + Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&3&1 \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&1&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 5}}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}}&0&3 \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{ - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then prove that {tex}{A^n} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos n\theta }&{\sin n\theta } \\ { - \sin n\theta }&{\cos n\theta } \end{array}} \right],\;n \in N{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then prove that {tex}{A^n} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos n\theta }&{\sin n\theta } \\ { - \sin n\theta }&{\cos n\theta } \end{array}} \right],\;n \in N{/tex}.
solution_text: We shall prove the result by the principle of mathematical induction. We have P(n) : If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then {tex}{A^n} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos n\theta }&{\sin n\theta } \\ { - \sin n\theta }&{\cos n\theta } \end{array}} \right],\;n \in N{/tex} P(1) : {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , so {tex}{A^1} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, the result is true for n = 1. Let the result be true for n = k. So P(k) : {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then {tex}{A^k} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos k\theta }&{\sin k\theta } \\ { - \sin k\theta }&{\cos k\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, we prove that the rsult holds for n = k +1 Now, {tex}{A^{k + 1}} = A \cdot {A^k}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos k\theta }&{\sin k\theta } \\ { - \sin k\theta }&{\cos k\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta \cos k\theta - \sin \theta \sin k\theta }&{\cos \theta \sin k\theta + \sin \theta \cos k\theta } \\ { - \sin \theta \cos k\theta + \cos \theta \sin k\theta }&{ - \sin \theta \sin k\theta + \cos \theta \cos k\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (\theta + k\theta )}&{\sin (\theta + k\theta )} \\ { - \sin (\theta + k\theta )}&{\cos (\theta + k\theta )} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (k + 1)\theta }&{\sin (k + 1)\theta } \\ { - \sin (k + 1)\theta }&{\cos (k + 1)\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, the result is true for n = k + 1. Thus by principle of mathematical induction. We have {tex}{A^n} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos n\theta }&{\sin n\theta } \\ { - \sin n\theta }&{\cos n\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , holds for all natural numbers.
solution_html:
We shall prove the result by the principle of mathematical induction.
We have P(n) : If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then {tex}{A^n} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos n\theta }&{\sin n\theta } \\ { - \sin n\theta }&{\cos n\theta } \end{array}} \right],\;n \in N{/tex}
P(1) : {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, so {tex}{A^1} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Therefore, the result is true for n = 1.
Let the result be true for n = k. So
P(k) : {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then {tex}{A^k} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos k\theta }&{\sin k\theta } \\ { - \sin k\theta }&{\cos k\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, we prove that the rsult holds for n = k +1
Now, {tex}{A^{k + 1}} = A \cdot {A^k}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta }&{\sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta }&{\cos \theta } \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos k\theta }&{\sin k\theta } \\ { - \sin k\theta }&{\cos k\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \theta \cos k\theta - \sin \theta \sin k\theta }&{\cos \theta \sin k\theta + \sin \theta \cos k\theta } \\ { - \sin \theta \cos k\theta + \cos \theta \sin k\theta }&{ - \sin \theta \sin k\theta + \cos \theta \cos k\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (\theta + k\theta )}&{\sin (\theta + k\theta )} \\ { - \sin (\theta + k\theta )}&{\cos (\theta + k\theta )} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (k + 1)\theta }&{\sin (k + 1)\theta } \\ { - \sin (k + 1)\theta }&{\cos (k + 1)\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Therefore, the result is true for n = k + 1. Thus by principle of mathematical induction.
We have {tex}{A^n} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos n\theta }&{\sin n\theta } \\ { - \sin n\theta }&{\cos n\theta } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, holds for all natural numbers.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A and B are symmetric matrices of the same order, then show that AB is symmetric if and only if A and B commute, that is AB = BA.
question_html: If A and B are symmetric matrices of the same order, then show that AB is symmetric if and only if A and B commute, that is AB = BA.
solution_text: Since A and B are both symmetric matrices, therefore A′ = A and B′ = B. Let AB be symmetric, then (AB)′ = AB But (AB)′ = B′A′ = BA Therefore BA = AB Conversely, if AB = BA, then we shall show that AB is symmetric. Now (AB)′ = B′A′ = B A ...(as A and B are symmetric) = AB Hence AB is symmetric if and only if A and B commute, that is AB = BA
solution_html:
Since A and B are both symmetric matrices, therefore A′ = A and B′ = B.
Let AB be symmetric, then (AB)′ = AB
But (AB)′ = B′A′ = BA 
Therefore BA = AB
Conversely, if AB = BA, then we shall show that AB is symmetric.
Now (AB)′ = B′A′
= B A ...(as A and B are symmetric)
= AB
Hence AB is symmetric if and only if A and B commute, that is AB = BA

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \\ 3&4 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 7&4 \end{array}} \right],\;C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&5 \\ 3&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} . Find a matrix D such that CD - AB = 0
question_html: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \\ 3&4 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 7&4 \end{array}} \right],\;C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&5 \\ 3&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}. Find a matrix D such that CD - AB = 0
solution_text: Let {tex}D = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Given that CD - AB = O {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&5 \\ 3&8 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \\ 3&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 7&4 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5c}&{2b + 5d} \\ {3a + 8c}&{3b + 8d} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&0 \\ {43}&{22} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5c - 3}&{2b + 5d} \\ {3a + 8c - 43}&{3b + 8d - 22} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} 2a + 5c – 3 = 0........(1) 2b + 5d = 0.........(2) 3a + 8c – 43 = 0..........(3) 3b + 8d – 22 = 0..........(4) Solving (1) and (3), we get, a = -191, c = 77. Solving (2) and (4), we get, b = -110, d = 44 {tex}D = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 191}&{ - 110} \\ {77}&{44} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}D = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Given that CD - AB = O
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&5 \\ 3&8 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \\ 3&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&2 \\ 7&4 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5c}&{2b + 5d} \\ {3a + 8c}&{3b + 8d} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&0 \\ {43}&{22} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5c - 3}&{2b + 5d} \\ {3a + 8c - 43}&{3b + 8d - 22} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
2a + 5c – 3 = 0........(1)
2b + 5d = 0.........(2)
3a + 8c – 43 = 0..........(3)
3b + 8d – 22 = 0..........(4)
Solving (1) and (3), we get, a = -191, c = 77.
Solving (2) and (4), we get, b = -110, d = 44
{tex}D = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 191}&{ - 110} \\ {77}&{44} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: In the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {2} & {5} & {19} & {-7} \\ {35} & {-2} & {\frac{5}{2}} & {12} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {1} & {-5} & {17} \end{array}\right]{/tex} write: The order of the matrix, The number of elements, Write the elements a 13 , a 21 , a 33 , a 24 , a 23 .
question_html:
In the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {2} & {5} & {19} & {-7} \\ {35} & {-2} & {\frac{5}{2}} & {12} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {1} & {-5} & {17} \end{array}\right]{/tex} write:

  1. The order of the matrix,
  2. The number of elements,
  3. Write the elements a13, a21, a33, a24, a23.
solution_text: In the given matrix, the number of rows is 3 and the number of columns is 4. Order of a matrix = No. of rows {tex}\times{/tex} No of columns Therefore, Order of the given matrix is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4. Since, the order of the matrix is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4, there are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 12 elements in it. {tex}a_{13}=19, a_{21}=35, a_{33}=-5, a_{24}=12, a_{23}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In the given matrix, the number of rows is 3 and the number of columns is 4.
    Order of a matrix = No. of rows {tex}\times{/tex} No of columns
    Therefore, Order of the given matrix is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.
  2. Since, the order of the matrix is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4, there are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 12 elements in it.
  3. {tex}a_{13}=19, a_{21}=35, a_{33}=-5, a_{24}=12, a_{23}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If a matrix has 24 elements, what are possible orders it can have? What, if it has 13 elements?
question_html: If a matrix has 24 elements, what are possible orders it can have? What, if it has 13 elements?
solution_text: Since, a matrix having mn element is of order m × n. Therefore, there are 8 possible matrices having 24 elements of orders 1 × 24, 2 × 12, 3 × 8, 4 × 6, 24 × 1, 12 × 2, 8 × 3, 6 × 4. Prime number 13 = 1 × 13 and 13 × 1. Therefore, there are 2 possible matrices of order 1 × 13 (Row matrix) and 13 × 1 (Column matrix).
solution_html:
Since, a matrix having mn element is of order m × n.

  1. Therefore, there are 8 possible matrices having 24 elements of orders 1 × 24, 2 × 12, 3 × 8, 4 × 6, 24 × 1, 12 × 2, 8 × 3, 6 × 4.
  2. Prime number 13 = 1 × 13 and 13 × 1. Therefore, there are 2 possible matrices of order 1 × 13 (Row matrix) and 13 × 1 (Column matrix).

question_text: If a matrix has 18 elements, what are the possible orders it can have? What if it has 5 elements?
question_html: If a matrix has 18 elements, what are the possible orders it can have? What if it has 5 elements?
solution_text: Since, a matrix having mn element is of order m × n. Therefore, there are 6 possible matrices having 18 elements of orders 1×18, 2×9, 3×6, 18×1, 9×2, 6×3. Prime number 5 = 1×5 and 5×1. Therefore, there are 2 possible matrices of order 1×5 (Row matrix) and 5×1 (Column matrix).
solution_html:
Since, a matrix having mn element is of order m × n.

  1. Therefore, there are 6 possible matrices having 18 elements of orders 1×18, 2×9, 3×6, 18×1, 9×2, 6×3.
  2. Prime number 5 = 1×5 and 5×1. Therefore, there are 2 possible matrices of order 1×5 (Row matrix) and 5×1 (Column matrix).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [a ij ] whose element a ij is given by: {tex}\frac{(i+j)^{2}}{2}{/tex}
question_html: Construct a 2{tex}\times{/tex}2 matrix A = [aij] whose element aij is given by: {tex}\frac{(i+j)^{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Let A = [a ij ] 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 So, the elements in a {tex}2\times2{/tex} matrix are a 11 , a 12 , a 21 , a 22 , A = {tex}\begin{bmatrix}a_{11}&a_{12}\\a_{21}&a_{22}\end{bmatrix}{/tex} ...(i) a 11 = {tex}\frac{\left(1+1\right)^2}2{/tex} = {tex}\frac42{/tex} = 2 a 12 = {tex}\frac{(1+2)^{2}}{2}=\frac{3^{2}}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} a 21 = {tex}\frac{(2+1)^{2}}{2}=\frac{3^{2}}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} a 22 = {tex}\frac{(2+2)^{2}}{2}=\frac{4^{2}}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8 So, (i) becomes A = {tex}\left(\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {\frac 92} \\ {\frac92} & {8} \end{array}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let A = [aij]2{tex}\times{/tex}2
So, the elements in a {tex}2\times2{/tex} matrix are
a11, a12, a21, a22,
A =  {tex}\begin{bmatrix}a_{11}&a_{12}\\a_{21}&a_{22}\end{bmatrix}{/tex} ...(i)
a11 ={tex}\frac{\left(1+1\right)^2}2{/tex}  = {tex}\frac42{/tex} = 2
a12 = {tex}\frac{(1+2)^{2}}{2}=\frac{3^{2}}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} 
a21 = {tex}\frac{(2+1)^{2}}{2}=\frac{3^{2}}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex}
a22 = {tex}\frac{(2+2)^{2}}{2}=\frac{4^{2}}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8
So, (i) becomes
A = {tex}\left(\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {\frac 92} \\ {\frac92} & {8} \end{array}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix, A = [aij], whose element {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{i}{j}{/tex}
question_html: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix, A = [aij], whose element {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{i}{j}{/tex}
solution_text: In general, a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is given by A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {a_{11}} & {a_{12}} \\ {a_{21}} & {a_{22}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}a_{i j}=\frac{i}{j}; ~~~ i, j=1,2{/tex} Therefore, {tex}a_{11}=\frac{1}{1}=1{/tex} {tex}a_{12}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{21}=\frac{2}{1}=2{/tex} {tex}a_{22}=\frac{2}{2}=1{/tex} Therefore, the required matrix is A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
In general, a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix is given by A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {a_{11}} & {a_{12}} \\ {a_{21}} & {a_{22}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}a_{i j}=\frac{i}{j}; ~~~ i, j=1,2{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}a_{11}=\frac{1}{1}=1{/tex}
{tex}a_{12}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a_{21}=\frac{2}{1}=2{/tex}
{tex}a_{22}=\frac{2}{2}=1{/tex}
Therefore, the required matrix is A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [a ij ], whose element a ij = {tex}\frac{(i+2j)^{2}}{2}{/tex} .
question_html: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [aij], whose element aij = {tex}\frac{(i+2j)^{2}}{2}{/tex}.
solution_text: Since it is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix it has 2 rows and 2 column. Let matrix be A Where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now it is given that {tex}\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\frac{(\mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j})^2}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{11}=\frac{(1+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(3)^2}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{12}=\frac{(1+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(5)^2}{2}=\frac{25}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{a}_{21}=\frac{(2+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(4)^2}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\mathrm{a}_{22}=\frac{(2+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(6)^2}{2}=\frac{36}{2}{/tex} = 18 Hence, the required matrix A is {tex}A\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{9}{2} & \frac{25}{2} \\ 8 & 18 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Since it is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix
it has 2 rows and 2 column.
Let matrix be A
Where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now it is given that
{tex}\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\frac{(\mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j})^2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a_{11}=\frac{(1+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(3)^2}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a_{12}=\frac{(1+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(5)^2}{2}=\frac{25}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{a}_{21}=\frac{(2+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(4)^2}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8
{tex}\mathrm{a}_{22}=\frac{(2+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(6)^2}{2}=\frac{36}{2}{/tex} = 18
Hence,
the required matrix A is
{tex}A\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{9}{2} & \frac{25}{2} \\ 8 & 18 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Construct a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix, whose elements are given by aij {tex} = \frac{1}{2}\left| { - 3i + j} \right|{/tex}
question_html: Construct a 3{tex}\times{/tex}4 matrix, whose elements are given by aij {tex} = \frac{1}{2}\left| { - 3i + j} \right|{/tex}
solution_html:
3{tex}\times{/tex}4 matrix is given by {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{a_{11}}} \\ {{a_{21}}} \\ {{a_{31}}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{a_{12}}} \\ {{a_{22}}} \\ {{a_{32}}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{a_{13}}} \\ {{a_{23}}} \\ {{a_{33}}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{a_{14}}} \\ {{a_{24}}} \\ {{a_{34}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Here, {tex}{a_{ij}} = \frac{1}{2}\left| { - 3i + j} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;{a_{11}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 1 + 1| = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 + 1|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}| - 2| = \frac{2}{2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}{a_{21}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 2 + 1| = \frac{1}{2}| - 6 + 1| = \frac{1}{2}| - 5| = \frac{5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{31}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 3 + 1| = \frac{1}{2}| - 9 + 1|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}| - 8| = \frac{8}{2} = 4{/tex}
{tex}{a_{12}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 1 + 2| = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 + 2|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}| - 1| = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{22}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 2 + 2| = \frac{1}{2}| - 6 + 2|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}| - 4| = \frac{4}{2} = 2{/tex}
{tex}{a_{32}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 3 + 2| = \frac{1}{2}| - 9 + 2|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}| - 7| = \frac{7}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{13}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 1 + 3| = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 + 3| = 0{/tex}
{tex}{a_{23}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 2 + 3| = \frac{1}{2}| - 6 + 3|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}| - 3| = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{33}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 3 + 3| = \frac{1}{2}| - 9 + 3|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}| - 6| = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex}
{tex}{a_{14}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 1 + 4| = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 + 4| = \frac{1}{2}|1| = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{24}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 2 + 4| = \frac{1}{2}| - 6 + 4|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}| - 2| = \frac{2}{2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}{a_{34}} = \frac{1}{2}| - 3 \times 3 + 4| = \frac{1}{2}| - 9 + 4|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}| - 5| = \frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, the required matrix is {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ {\frac{5}{2}} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}} \\ 2 \\ {\frac{7}{2}} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ {\frac{3}{2}} \\ 3 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}} \\ 1 \\ {\frac{5}{2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Construct a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix, whose elements are given by a ij = 2i - j
question_html: Construct a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix, whose elements are given by aij = 2i - j
solution_text: In general 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix is given by A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{llll} {a_{11}} & {a_{12}} & {a_{13}} & {a_{14}} \\ {a_{21}} & {a_{22}} & {a_{23}} & {a_{24}} \\ {a_{31}} & {a_{32}} & {a_{33}} & {a_{34}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} a ij = 2i - j, i = 1, 2, 3 and j = 1, 2, 3, 4 Therefore, a 11 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1 a 21 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3 a 31 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5 a 12 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 2 = 2 - 2 = 0 a 22 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 2 = 4 - 2 = 2 a 32 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 2 = 6 - 2 = 4 a 13 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 3 = 2 - 3 = -1 a 23 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 3 = 4 - 3 = 1 a 33 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 3 = 6 - 3 = 3 a 14 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 = 2 - 4 = -2 a 24 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 4 = 4 - 4 = 0 a 34 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 4 = 6 - 4 = 2 Therefore, required matrix is A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1} & {0} & {-1} & {-2} \\ {3} & {2} & {1} & {0} \\ {5} & {4} & {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
In general 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix is given by A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{llll} {a_{11}} & {a_{12}} & {a_{13}} & {a_{14}} \\ {a_{21}} & {a_{22}} & {a_{23}} & {a_{24}} \\ {a_{31}} & {a_{32}} & {a_{33}} & {a_{34}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
aij = 2i - j, i = 1, 2, 3 and j = 1, 2, 3, 4
Therefore,
a11 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1
a21 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3
a31 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5
a12 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 2 = 2 - 2 = 0
a22 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 2 = 4 - 2 = 2
a32 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 2 = 6 - 2 = 4
a13 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 3 = 2 - 3 = -1
a23 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 3 = 4 - 3 = 1
a33 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 3 = 6 - 3 = 3
a14 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 4 = 2 - 4 = -2
a24 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 4 = 4 - 4 = 0
a34 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 - 4 = 6 - 4 = 2
Therefore, required matrix is A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cccc} {1} & {0} & {-1} & {-2} \\ {3} & {2} & {1} & {0} \\ {5} & {4} & {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of x, y, and z from the following equation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {3} \\ {x} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {y} & {z} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the values of x, y, and z from the following equation:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {3} \\ {x} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {y} & {z} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {3} \\ {x} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {y} & {z} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal. Comparing the corresponding element, we have: x = 1, y = 4 and z = 3
solution_html:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {3} \\ {x} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {y} & {z} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal.
Comparing the corresponding element, we have:
x = 1, y = 4 and z = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of x, y, and z from the following equation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {x+y} & {2} \\ {5+z} & {x y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {2} \\ {5} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the values of x, y, and z from the following equation:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {x+y} & {2} \\ {5+z} & {x y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {2} \\ {5} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal. Comparing the corresponding element, we have: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {x+y} & {2} \\ {5+z} & {x y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {2} \\ {5} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex} x + y = 6, xy = 8, 5 + z = 5 Now, 5 + z = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 0 x+ y = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 - y Also, xy = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - y)y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y - y 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 - 6y + 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 - 4y - 2y + 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(y - 4) - 2(y - 4) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 2)(y - 4) = 0 Hence, y = 2 or y = 4 if y = 2, then x = 6 - 2 = 4 if y = 4, then x = 6 - 4 = 2
solution_html:
Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal.
Comparing the corresponding element, we have:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {x+y} & {2} \\ {5+z} & {x y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {2} \\ {5} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
x + y = 6, xy = 8, 5 + z = 5
Now, 5 + z = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 0
x+ y = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 - y
Also, xy = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6 - y)y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6y - y2 = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 - 6y  + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 - 4y - 2y + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(y - 4) - 2(y - 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 2)(y - 4) = 0
Hence, y = 2 or y = 4
if y = 2, then x = 6 - 2 = 4
if y = 4, then x = 6 - 4 = 2

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of x, y, and z from the following equation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {x+y+z} \\ {x+z} \\ {y+z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {9} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the values of x, y, and z from the following equation:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {x+y+z} \\ {x+z} \\ {y+z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {9} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {x+y+z} \\ {x+z} \\ {y+z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {9} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal. Comparing the corresponding element, we have: x + y + z = 9 ....(1) x + z = 5....(2) y + z = 7....(3) Putting the value of equation (2) in equation (1), gives y + 5 = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 Next, putting the value of y in equation 3, we get 4 + z = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 3 Finally, x + z = 5, gives x + 3 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 Therefore, x = 2, y = 4 and z = 3.
solution_html:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {x+y+z} \\ {x+z} \\ {y+z} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {9} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal.
Comparing the corresponding element, we have:
x + y + z = 9 ....(1)
x + z = 5....(2)
y + z = 7....(3)
Putting the value of equation (2) in equation (1), gives
y + 5 = 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4
Next, putting the value of y in equation 3, we get
4 + z = 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 3
Finally, x + z = 5, gives
x + 3 = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
Therefore, x = 2, y = 4 and z = 3.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of a, b, c and d from the equation {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a - b}&{2a + c} \\ {2a - b}&{3c + d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&5 \\ 0&{13} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find the values of a, b, c and d from the equation {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a - b}&{2a + c} \\ {2a - b}&{3c + d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&5 \\ 0&{13} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: Equating corresponding entries, a - b = -1 ……….(i) 2a - b = 0 ……….(ii) 2a + c = 5 ……….(iii) 3c + d = 13 ……….(iv) Eq. (i) – Eq. (ii) = -a = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 Putting a = 1 in eq. (i), 1 - b = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -b = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2 Putting a = 1 in eq. (iii), 2 + c = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 5 - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 3 Putting c = 3 in eq. (iv), 9 + d = 13 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 13 - 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1, b = 2, c = 3, d = 4
solution_html:
Equating corresponding entries,
a - b = -1 ……….(i)
2a - b = 0 ……….(ii)
2a + c = 5 ……….(iii)
3c + d = 13 ……….(iv)
Eq. (i) – Eq. (ii) = -a = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
Putting a = 1 in eq. (i), 1 - b = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -b = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2
Putting a = 1 in eq. (iii), 2 + c = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 5 - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 3
Putting c = 3 in eq. (iv), 9 + d = 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 13 - 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1, b = 2, c = 3, d = 4

question_text: A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n is a square matrix, if
question_html: A = [aij] m {tex}\times{/tex} n is a square matrix, if

options.explanation: We know that if a given matrix is said to be a square matrix if the number of rows is equal to the number of columns. Therefore, A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n is a square matrix if m = n.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Which of the given values of x and y make the pair of matrices equal {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Which of the given values of x and y make the pair of matrices equal
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

options.explanation: In the given question: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3 x+7} & {5} \\ {y+1} & {2-3 x} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {y-2} \\ {8} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, the given matrices are equal, their corresponding elements are also equal. Comparing the corresponding element, we have: 3x + 7 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=-\frac{7}{3}{/tex} Also, 5 = y – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 Again, y + 1 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 And 2 – 3x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow ~x=-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} Therefore, on comparing the corresponding elements of the two matrices, we get different values of x, which is not possible. Thus, it is not possible to find the values of x and y for which the given matrices are equal.

question_html: The number of all possible matrices of order {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} with each entry 0 or 1 is

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {3} \\ {-2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} . Find each of the following: A + B A - B 3A - C AB BA
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {3} \\ {-2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}. Find each of the following:

  1. A + B
  2. A - B
  3. 3A - C
  4. AB
  5. BA
solution_text: A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {-2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2+1} & {4+3} \\ {3-2} & {2+5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {7} \\ {1} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} A – B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {-2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2-1} & {4-3} \\ {3-(-2)} & {2-5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {1} \\ {5} & {-3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have, 3A - question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&{ - 3} \\ 5&0&2 \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&2&5 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1&2 \\ 0&3&2 \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then compute (A + B) and (B – C). Also, verify that A + (B - C) = (A + B) - C.
question_html:
If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&{ - 3} \\ 5&0&2 \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&2&5 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1&2 \\ 0&3&2 \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then compute (A + B) and (B – C).
Also, verify that A + (B - C) = (A + B) - C.
solution_html:
{tex}A + B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&{ - 3} \\ 5&0&2 \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&2&5 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 3}&{2 - 1}&{ - 3 + 2} \\ {5 + 4}&{0 + 2}&{2 + 5} \\ {1 + 2}&{ - 1 + 0}&{1 + 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1&{ - 1} \\ 9&2&7 \\ 3&{ - 1}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}B - C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&2&5 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1&2 \\ 0&3&2 \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3 - 4}&{ - 1 - 1}&{2 - 2} \\ {4 - 0}&{2 - 3}&{5 - 2} \\ {2 - 1}&{0 + 2}&{3 - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 2}&0 \\ 4&{ - 1}&3 \\ 1&2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, we show; A + (B – C) = (A + B) – C
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&{ - 3} \\ 5&0&2 \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 2}&0 \\ 4&{ - 1}&3 \\ 1&2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1&{ - 1} \\ 9&2&7 \\ 3&{ - 1}&4 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1&2 \\ 0&3&2 \\ 1&{ - 2}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - 1}&{2 - 2}&{ - 3 + 0} \\ {5 + 4}&{0 - 1}&{2 + 3} \\ {1 + 1}&{ - 1 + 2}&{1 + 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {4 - 4}&{1 - 1}&{ - 1 - 2} \\ {9 - 0}&{2 - 3}&{7 - 2} \\ {3 - 1}&{ - 1 + 2}&{4 - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&{ - 3} \\ 9&{ - 1}&5 \\ 2&1&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&{ - 3} \\ 9&{ - 1}&5 \\ 2&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S. Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{2}{3}}&1&{\frac{5}{3}} \\ {\frac{1}{3}}&{\frac{2}{3}}&{\frac{4}{3}} \\ {\frac{7}{3}}&2&{\frac{2}{3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{2}{5}}&{\frac{3}{5}}&1 \\ {\frac{1}{5}}&{\frac{2}{5}}&{\frac{4}{5}} \\ {\frac{7}{5}}&{\frac{6}{5}}&{\frac{2}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then compute 3A – 5B.
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{2}{3}}&1&{\frac{5}{3}} \\ {\frac{1}{3}}&{\frac{2}{3}}&{\frac{4}{3}} \\ {\frac{7}{3}}&2&{\frac{2}{3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{2}{5}}&{\frac{3}{5}}&1 \\ {\frac{1}{5}}&{\frac{2}{5}}&{\frac{4}{5}} \\ {\frac{7}{5}}&{\frac{6}{5}}&{\frac{2}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then compute 3A – 5B.
solution_html:
3A – 5B = {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{2}{3}}&1&{\frac{5}{3}} \\ {\frac{1}{3}}&{\frac{2}{3}}&{\frac{4}{3}} \\ {\frac{7}{3}}&2&{\frac{2}{3}} \end{array}} \right] - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{2}{5}}&{\frac{3}{5}}&1 \\ {\frac{1}{5}}&{\frac{2}{5}}&{\frac{4}{5}} \\ {\frac{7}{5}}&{\frac{6}{5}}&{\frac{2}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&5 \\ 1&2&4 \\ 7&6&2 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&5 \\ 1&2&4 \\ 7&6&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 - 2}&{3 - 3}&{5 - 5} \\ {1 - 1}&{2 - 2}&{4 - 4} \\ {7 - 7}&{6 - 6}&{2 - 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Simplify {tex}\cos \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] + \sin \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin \theta } & { - \cos \theta } \\ { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Simplify {tex}\cos \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] + \sin \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin \theta } & { - \cos \theta } \\ { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: According to the question, {tex}\cos \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] + \sin \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin \theta } & { - \cos \theta } \\ { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } & { \sin \theta \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta \cos \theta } & { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } & { - \sin \theta \cos \theta } \\ { \sin \theta \cos \theta } & { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } & { \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta \cos \theta + \sin \theta \cos \theta } & { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&{ 1} \end{array}} \right]\quad \left[ {\because {{\sin }^2}\theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta = 1} \right]{/tex} = I = unit matrix.
solution_html:
According to the question,

 {tex}\cos \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] + \sin \theta \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin \theta } & { - \cos \theta } \\ { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } & { \sin \theta \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta \cos \theta } & { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } & { - \sin \theta \cos \theta } \\ { \sin \theta \cos \theta } & { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } & { \sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta } \\ { - \sin \theta \cos \theta + \sin \theta \cos \theta } & { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&{ 1} \end{array}} \right]\quad \left[ {\because {{\sin }^2}\theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta = 1} \right]{/tex}
= I = unit matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find X and Y, if {tex}\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{X}-\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find X and Y, if {tex}\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{X}-\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Given that, X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .....(1) X - Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ....(2) Adding (1) and (2), we get {tex}2 X=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7+3} & {0+0} \\ {2+0} & {5+3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {10} & {0} \\ {2} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow X=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {10} & {0} \\ {2} & {8} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {0} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {0} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]+Y=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Y=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {0} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Y=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .....(1)
X - Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ....(2)
Adding (1) and (2), we get
{tex}2 X=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7+3} & {0+0} \\ {2+0} & {5+3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
