 {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {10} & {0} \\ {2} & {8} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow X=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {10} & {0} \\ {2} & {8} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {0} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, X + Y =  {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {0} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]+Y=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Y=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {0} \\ {2} & {5} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {0} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Y=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find X and Y, if {tex}2 \mathrm{X}+3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right] \text { and } 3 \mathrm{X}+2 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {-2} \\ {-1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find X and Y, if {tex}2 \mathrm{X}+3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right] \text { and } 3 \mathrm{X}+2 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {-2} \\ {-1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: 2X + 3Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ......(1) 3X + 2Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {-2} \\ {-1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .....(2) Now, multiply equation (1) by 2 and equation (2) by 3, we get, 4X + 6Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {6} \\ {8} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .....(3) 9X + 6Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-6} \\ {-3} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ....(4) Subtracting equation (4) from (3), we get, (4X + 6Y) – (9X + 6Y) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {6} \\ {8} & {0} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-6} \\ {-3} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-5 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4-6} & {6-(-6)} \\ {8-(-3)} & {0-15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {12} \\ {11} & {-15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{X}=-\frac{1}{5}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {12} \\ {11} & {-15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2}{5}} & {\frac{-12}{5}} \\ {\frac{-11}{5}} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, 2X + 3Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2}{5}} & {\frac{-12}{5}} \\ {\frac{-11}{5}} & {3} \end{array}\right]+3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{4}{5}} & {\frac{-24}{5}} \\ {\frac{-22}{5}} & {6} \end{array}\right]+3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{4}{5}} & {\frac{-24}{5}} \\ {\frac{-22}{5}} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2-\frac{4}{5}} & {3+\frac{24}{5}} \\ {4+\frac{22}{5}} & {0-6} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{6}{5}} & {\frac{39}{5}} \\ {\frac{42}{5}} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Y=\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{6}{5}} & {\frac{39}{5}} \\ {\frac{42}{5}} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Y=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2}{5}} & {\frac{13}{5}} \\ {\frac{14}{5}} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
2X + 3Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ......(1)
3X + 2Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {-2} \\ {-1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .....(2)
Now, multiply equation (1) by 2 and equation (2) by 3, we get,
4X + 6Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {6} \\ {8} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .....(3)
9X + 6Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-6} \\ {-3} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ....(4)
Subtracting equation (4) from (3), we get,
(4X + 6Y) – (9X + 6Y) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {6} \\ {8} & {0} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-6} \\ {-3} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-5 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4-6} & {6-(-6)} \\ {8-(-3)} & {0-15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {12} \\ {11} & {-15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{X}=-\frac{1}{5}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {12} \\ {11} & {-15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2}{5}} & {\frac{-12}{5}} \\ {\frac{-11}{5}} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, 2X + 3Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2}{5}} & {\frac{-12}{5}} \\ {\frac{-11}{5}} & {3} \end{array}\right]+3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{4}{5}} & {\frac{-24}{5}} \\ {\frac{-22}{5}} & {6} \end{array}\right]+3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {0} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{4}{5}} & {\frac{-24}{5}} \\ {\frac{-22}{5}} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2-\frac{4}{5}} & {3+\frac{24}{5}} \\ {4+\frac{22}{5}} & {0-6} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{6}{5}} & {\frac{39}{5}} \\ {\frac{42}{5}} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Y=\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{6}{5}} & {\frac{39}{5}} \\ {\frac{42}{5}} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Y=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2}{5}} & {\frac{13}{5}} \\ {\frac{14}{5}} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find X, if Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right] \text { and } 2 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find X, if Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right] \text { and } 2 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: 2X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-3} & {0-2} \\ {-3-1} & {2-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {-2} \\ {-4} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow X=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {-2} & {-2} \\ {-4} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow X=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
2X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {0} \\ {-3} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {2} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-3} & {0-2} \\ {-3-1} & {2-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {-2} \\ {-4} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow X=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {-2} & {-2} \\ {-4} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow X=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find x and y, if {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&x \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y&0 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find x and y, if {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&x \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y&0 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&x \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y&0 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&6 \\ 0&{2x} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y&0 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + y}&6 \\ 1&{2x + 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have 2 + y = 5 and 2x + 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5 - 2 and 2(x + 1) = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 and x + 1 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 and x = 3
solution_html:
Given: {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&x \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y&0 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&6 \\ 0&{2x} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y&0 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + y}&6 \\ 1&{2x + 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&6 \\ 1&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries, we have
2 + y = 5 and 2x + 2 = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5 - 2 and 2(x + 1) = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 and x + 1 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 and x = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the equation for x, y, z and t if {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&z \\ y&t \end{array}} \right] + 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right] = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \\ 4&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Solve the equation for x, y, z and t if {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&z \\ y&t \end{array}} \right] + 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right] = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \\ 4&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&z \\ y&t \end{array}} \right] + 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right] = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \\ 4&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&{2z} \\ {2y}&{2t} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 3} \\ 0&6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&{15} \\ {12}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + 3}&{2z - 3} \\ {2y + 0}&{2t + 6} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&{15} \\ {12}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have 2x + 3 = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 9 - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 and 2z - 3 = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z = 15 + 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 9 and 2y = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 6 And 2t + 6 = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2t = 18 - 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2t = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 6 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3, y = 6, z = 9, t = 6
solution_html:
Given: {tex}2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&z \\ y&t \end{array}} \right] + 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right] = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \\ 4&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&{2z} \\ {2y}&{2t} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 3} \\ 0&6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&{15} \\ {12}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + 3}&{2z - 3} \\ {2y + 0}&{2t + 6} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&{15} \\ {12}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries, we have
2x + 3 = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 9 - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3
and 2z - 3 = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z = 15 + 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 9
and 2y = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 6
And 2t + 6 = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2t = 18 - 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2t = 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3, y = 6, z = 9, t = 6

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , find the values of x and y.
question_html: If {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, find the values of x and y.
solution_text: Given: {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x} \\ {3x} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - y} \\ y \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x - y} \\ {3x + y} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have 2x - y = 10 ……….(i) and 3x + y = 5 ……….(ii) Adding eq. (i) and (ii), we have 5x = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 Putting x = 3 in eq. (ii), 9 + y = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -4
solution_html:
Given: {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x} \\ {3x} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - y} \\ y \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x - y} \\ {3x + y} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries, we have
2x - y = 10 ……….(i) and 3x + y = 5 ……….(ii)
Adding eq. (i) and (ii), we have 5x = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3
Putting x = 3 in eq. (ii), 9 + y = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -4

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Given: {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y \\ z&w \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&6 \\ { - 1}&{2w} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{x + y} \\ {z + w}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , find the values of x, y, z and w.
question_html: Given: {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y \\ z&w \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&6 \\ { - 1}&{2w} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{x + y} \\ {z + w}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, find the values of x, y, z and w.
solution_text: Given: {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y \\ z&w \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&6 \\ { - 1}&{2w} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{x + y} \\ {z + w}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3x}&{3y} \\ {3z}&{3w} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + 4}&{6 + x + y} \\ { - 1 + z + w}&{2w + 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have 3x = x + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 and 3y = 6 + x + y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 6 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4 and 3z = -1 + z + w {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z - w = - 1 ….(i) and 3w = 2w + 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} w = 3 Putting w = 3 in eq. (i), 2z - 3 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = 4, z = 1, w = 3
solution_html:
Given: {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y \\ z&w \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&6 \\ { - 1}&{2w} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{x + y} \\ {z + w}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3x}&{3y} \\ {3z}&{3w} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x + 4}&{6 + x + y} \\ { - 1 + z + w}&{2w + 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries, we have
3x = x + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
and    3y = 6 + x + y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 6 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 4
and 3z = -1 + z + w {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z - w = - 1 ….(i)
and 3w = 2w + 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} w = 3
Putting w = 3 in eq. (i), 2z - 3 = -1 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2z = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = 4, z = 1, w = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}F(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{ - \sin x}&0 \\ {\sin x}&{\cos x}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , show that F(x)F(y) = F(x + y).
question_html: If {tex}F(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{ - \sin x}&0 \\ {\sin x}&{\cos x}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, show that F(x)F(y) = F(x + y).
solution_text: {tex}F(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{ - \sin x}&0 \\ {\sin x}&{\cos x}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , {tex}F(y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{ - \sin y}&0 \\ {\sin y}&{\cos y}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}F(x + y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{ - \sin (x + y)}&0 \\ {\sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} F(x)F(y) {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{ - \sin x}&0 \\ {\sin x}&{\cos x}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{ - \sin y}&0 \\ {\sin y}&{\cos y}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x\cos y - \sin x\sin y + 0}&{ - \cos x\sin y - \sin x\cos y + 0}&0 \\ {\sin x\cos y + \cos x\sin y + 0}&{ - \sin x\sin y + \cos x\cos y + 0}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{ - \sin (x + y)}&0 \\ {\sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = F(x + y) {tex}\therefore{/tex} F(x)F(y) = F(x + y)
solution_html:
{tex}F(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{ - \sin x}&0 \\ {\sin x}&{\cos x}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, {tex}F(y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{ - \sin y}&0 \\ {\sin y}&{\cos y}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}F(x + y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{ - \sin (x + y)}&0 \\ {\sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
F(x)F(y){tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{ - \sin x}&0 \\ {\sin x}&{\cos x}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{ - \sin y}&0 \\ {\sin y}&{\cos y}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x\cos y - \sin x\sin y + 0}&{ - \cos x\sin y - \sin x\cos y + 0}&0 \\ {\sin x\cos y + \cos x\sin y + 0}&{ - \sin x\sin y + \cos x\cos y + 0}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{ - \sin (x + y)}&0 \\ {\sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
= F(x + y)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F(x)F(y) = F(x + y)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right] \neq\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Show that {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right] \neq\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: L.H.S = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5(2)-1(3)} & {5(1)-1(4)} \\ {6(2)+7(3)} & {6(1)+7(4)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {10-3} & {5-4} \\ {12+21} & {6+28} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {7} & {1} \\ {33} & {34} \end{array}\right]~...(i){/tex} R.H.S = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2(5)+1(6)} & {2(-1)+1(7)} \\ {3(5)+4(6)} & {3(-1)+4(7)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {10+6} & {-2+7} \\ {15+24} & {-3+28} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {16} & {5} \\ {39} & {25} \end{array}\right]~...(ii){/tex} Therefore, from (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right] \neq\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
L.H.S ={tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5(2)-1(3)} & {5(1)-1(4)} \\ {6(2)+7(3)} & {6(1)+7(4)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {10-3} & {5-4} \\ {12+21} & {6+28} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {7} & {1} \\ {33} & {34} \end{array}\right]~...(i){/tex}
R.H.S = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2(5)+1(6)} & {2(-1)+1(7)} \\ {3(5)+4(6)} & {3(-1)+4(7)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {10+6} & {-2+7} \\ {15+24} & {-3+28} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {16} & {5} \\ {39} & {25} \end{array}\right]~...(ii){/tex}
Therefore, from (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right] \neq\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {1} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {5} & {-1} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right] \neq\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have,

{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1(-1)+2(0)+3(2)} & {1(1)+2(-1)+3(3)} & {1(0)+2(1)+3(4)} \\ {0(-1)+1(0)+0(2)} & {0(1)+1(-1)+0(3)} & {0(0)+1(1)+0(4)} \\ {1(-1)+1(0)+0(2)} & {1(1)+1(-1)+0(3)} & {1(0)+1(1)+0(4)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {8} & {14} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

Now, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1(1)+1(0)+0(1)} & {-1(2)+1(1)+0(1)} & {-1(3)+1(0)+0(0)} \\ {0(1)+(-1)(0)+1(1)} & {0(2)+(-1)(1)+1(1)} & {0(3)+)(-1)(0)+1(0)} \\ {2(1)+3(0)+4(1)} & {2(2)+3(1)+4(1)} & {2(3)+3(0)+4(0)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {-1} & {-3} \\ {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {6} & {11} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right] \neq\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find A 2 - 5A + 6I if {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&1 \\ 2&1&3 \\ 1&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find A2 - 5A + 6I if {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&1 \\ 2&1&3 \\ 1&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_html:
A2 - 5A + 6I {tex}=\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&1 \\ 2&1&3 \\ 1&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&1 \\ 2&1&3 \\ 1&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&1 \\ 2&1&3 \\ 1&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] + 6\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {4 + 0 + 1}&{0 + 0 - 1}&{2 + 0 + 0} \\ {4 + 2 + 3}&{0 + 1 - 3}&{2 + 3 + 0} \\ {2 - 2 + 0}&{0 - 1 - 0}&{1 - 3 + 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&0&5 \\ {10}&5&{15} \\ 5&{ - 5}&0 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0&0 \\ 0&6&0 \\ 0&0&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&{ - 1}&2 \\ 9&{ - 2}&5 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&0&5 \\ {10}&5&{15} \\ 5&{ - 5}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0&0 \\ 0&6&0 \\ 0&0&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {5 - 10 + 6}&{ - 1 - 0 + 0}&{2 - 5 + 0} \\ {9 - 10 + 0}&{ - 2 - 5 + 6}&{5 - 15 + 0} \\ {0 - 5 + 0}&{ - 1 + 5 + 0}&{ - 2 + 0 + 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&{ - 3} \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&{ - 10} \\ { - 5}&4&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , prove that A 3 - 6A 2 + 7A + 2I = 0.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, prove that A3 - 6A2 + 7A + 2I = 0.
solution_html:
L.H.S. = A3 - 6A2 + 7A + 2I
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - 6\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 0 + 4}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{2 + 0 + 6} \\ {0 + 0 + 2}&{0 + 4 + 0}&{0 + 2 + 3} \\ {2 + 0 + 6}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{4 + 0 + 9} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - 6\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 0 + 4}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{2 + 0 + 6} \\ {0 + 0 + 2}&{0 + 4 + 0}&{0 + 2 + 3} \\ {2 + 0 + 6}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{4 + 0 + 9} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0&{14} \\ 0&{14}&7 \\ {14}&0&{21} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&0 \\ 0&2&0 \\ 0&0&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {5 + 0 + 16}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{10 + 0 + 24} \\ {2 + 0 + 10}&{0 + 8 + 0}&{4 + 4 + 15} \\ {8 + 0 + 26}&{0 + 0 + 0}&{16 + 0 + 39} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30}&0&{48} \\ {12}&{24}&{30} \\ {48}&0&{78} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {7 + 2}&{0 + 0}&{14 + 0} \\ {0 + 0}&{14 + 2}&{7 + 0} \\ {14 + 0}&{0 + 0}&{21 + 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {21}&0&{34} \\ {12}&8&{23} \\ {34}&0&{55} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30}&0&{48} \\ {12}&{24}&{30} \\ {48}&0&{78} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0&{14} \\ 0&{16}&7 \\ {14}&0&{23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {21 - 30}&{0 - 0}&{34 - 48} \\ {12 - 12}&{8 - 24}&{23 - 30} \\ {34 - 48}&{0 - 0}&{55 - 78} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0&{14} \\ 0&{16}&7 \\ {14}&0&{23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9}&0&{ - 14} \\ 0&{ - 16}&{ - 7} \\ { - 14}&0&{ - 23} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&0&{14} \\ 0&{16}&7 \\ {14}&0&{23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9 + 9}&{0 + 0}&{ - 14 + 14} \\ {0 + 0}&{ - 16 + 16}&{ - 7 + 7} \\ { - 14 + 14}&{0 + 0}&{ - 23 + 23} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} (Zero matrix)
= R.H.S. Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} & {tex}I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ; Find k, So that A 2 = kA - 2I.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} & {tex}I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}; Find k, So that A2 = kA - 2I.
solution_text: A 2 = A.A {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 8}&{ - 6 + 4} \\ {12 - 8}&{ - 8 + 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} A 2 = kA - 2I. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right] = k\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] - 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3k}&{ - 2k} \\ {4k}&{ - 2k} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3k}&{ - 2k} \\ {4k}&{ - 2k} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3k = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1
solution_html:
A2 = A.A
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 8}&{ - 6 + 4} \\ {12 - 8}&{ - 8 + 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
A2 = kA - 2I.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right] = k\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] - 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3k}&{ - 2k} \\ {4k}&{ - 2k} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 2} \\ 4&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3k}&{ - 2k} \\ {4k}&{ - 2k} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3k = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and I is the identity matrix of order 2, show that {tex}I + A = (I - A)\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and I is the identity matrix of order 2, show that {tex}I + A = (I - A)\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: L.H.S. {tex}I + A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}I - A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ { - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} R.H.S. {tex} = (I - A)\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ { - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha + \sin \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&{ - \sin \alpha + \cos \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ { - \cos \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2} + \sin \alpha }&{\sin \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2} + \cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha + \sin \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{ - \sin \alpha + \cos \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ { - \cos \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}} + \sin \alpha }&{\sin \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}} + \cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2} + \sin \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2} + \cos \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{ - \cos \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2} + \sin \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\sin \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2} + \cos \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{\sin \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\cos \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S. Proved.
solution_html:
L.H.S. {tex}I + A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}I - A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ { - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
R.H.S. {tex} = (I - A)\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ { - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha + \sin \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&{ - \sin \alpha + \cos \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ { - \cos \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2} + \sin \alpha }&{\sin \alpha \tan \frac{\alpha }{2} + \cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha + \sin \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{ - \sin \alpha + \cos \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ { - \cos \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}} + \sin \alpha }&{\sin \alpha \frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}} + \cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2} + \sin \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2} + \cos \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{ - \cos \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2} + \sin \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\sin \alpha \sin \frac{\alpha }{2} + \cos \alpha \cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{\sin \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\cos \left( {\alpha - \frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{ - \sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \\ {\frac{{\sin \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}}&{\frac{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}{{\cos \frac{\alpha }{2}}}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - \tan \frac{\alpha }{2}} \\ {\tan \frac{\alpha }{2}}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S. Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A trust fund has ₹ 30,000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds. The first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year. Using matrix multiplication, determine how to divide ₹ 30,000 among the two types of bonds. If the trust fund must obtain an annual total interest of: ₹ 1800
question_html: A trust fund has ₹ 30,000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds. The first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year. Using matrix multiplication, determine how to divide ₹ 30,000 among the two types of bonds. If the trust fund must obtain an annual total interest of: ₹ 1800
solution_text: Let Rs x be invested in the first bond. Then, the sum of money invested in the second bond will be ₹ (30000 – x). It is given that the first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year. Thus, in order to obtain an annual total interest of ₹ 1800, we get: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {x} & {(30000-x)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {\frac{5}{100}} \\ {\frac{7}{100}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 1800 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5 \mathrm{x}}{100}+\frac{7(30000-\mathrm{x})}{100}{/tex} = 1800 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 210000 -7x = 180000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 210000 -2x = 180000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 210000 – 180000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 30000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15000 Therefore, in order to obtain an annual total interest of ₹ 1800, the trust fund should invest ₹ 15000 in the first bond and the remaining ₹ 15000 in the second bond.
solution_html:
Let Rs x be invested in the first bond.
Then, the sum of money invested in the second bond will be ₹ (30000 – x).
It is given that the first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year.
Thus, in order to obtain an annual total interest of ₹ 1800, we get:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {x} & {(30000-x)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {\frac{5}{100}} \\ {\frac{7}{100}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 1800
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5 \mathrm{x}}{100}+\frac{7(30000-\mathrm{x})}{100}{/tex} = 1800
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 210000 -7x = 180000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 210000 -2x = 180000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 210000 – 180000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 30000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15000
Therefore, in order to obtain an annual total interest of ₹ 1800, the trust fund should invest ₹ 15000 in the first bond and the remaining ₹ 15000 in the second bond.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A trust fund has ₹ 30,000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds. The first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year. Using matrix multiplication, determine how to divide ₹ 30,000 among the two types of bonds. If the trust fund must obtain an annual total interest of: ₹ 2000
question_html: A trust fund has ₹ 30,000 that must be invested in two different types of bonds. The first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year. Using matrix multiplication, determine how to divide ₹ 30,000 among the two types of bonds. If the trust fund must obtain an annual total interest of: ₹ 2000
solution_text: Let ₹ x be invested in the first bond. Then, the sum of money invested in the second bond will be ₹ (30000 – x). It is given that the first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year. Hence, the amount invested in each type of the bonds can be represented in matrix form with each column corresponding to a different type of bond as: {tex}X=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 30000-x \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, the interest obtained after one year can be expressed in matrix representation as: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {x} & {(30000-x)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {\frac{5}{100}} \\ {\frac{7}{100}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 2000 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5 x}{100}+\frac{7(30000-x)}{100}{/tex} = 2000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 210000 -7x = 200000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 210000 -2x = 200000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 210000 – 200000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 10000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5000 Therefore, in order to obtain an annual total interest of ₹ 2000, the trust fund should invest ₹ 5000 in the first bond and the remaining ₹ (30000−5000) = ₹ 25000 in the second bond.
solution_html:
Let ₹ x be invested in the first bond.
Then, the sum of money invested in the second bond will be ₹ (30000 – x).
It is given that the first bond pays 5% interest per year, and the second bond pays 7% interest per year.
Hence, the amount invested in each type of the bonds can be represented in matrix form with each column corresponding to a different type of bond as:
{tex}X=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 30000-x \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence, the interest obtained after one year can be expressed in matrix representation as:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {x} & {(30000-x)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {\frac{5}{100}} \\ {\frac{7}{100}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 2000
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5 x}{100}+\frac{7(30000-x)}{100}{/tex} = 2000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  5x + 210000 -7x = 200000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 210000 -2x = 200000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 210000 – 200000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 10000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5000
Therefore, in order to obtain an annual total interest of ₹ 2000, the trust fund should invest ₹ 5000 in the first bond and the remaining ₹ (30000−5000)  = ₹ 25000 in the second bond.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: The bookshop of a particular school has 10 dozen chemistry books, 8 dozen physics books, 10 dozen economics books. Their selling prices are Rs 80, Rs 60 and Rs 40 each respectively. Find the total amount the bookshop will receive from selling all the books using matrix algebra.
question_html: The bookshop of a particular school has 10 dozen chemistry books, 8 dozen physics books, 10 dozen economics books. Their selling prices are Rs 80, Rs 60 and Rs 40 each respectively. Find the total amount the bookshop will receive from selling all the books using matrix algebra.
solution_text: Let the number of books as a 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix= {tex}B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;\;10\;dozen}&{\;\;\;\;8\;dozen}&{\;\;\;\;10\;dozen} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10 \times 12 = 120}&{8 \times 12 = 96}&{10 \times 12 \times 120} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} Let the selling prices of each book as a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 matrix {tex}S = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80} \\ {60} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total amount received by selling all books = {tex}BS = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {120}&{96}&{120} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80} \\ {60} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = [120(80) + 96(60) + 120(40)] = [9,600 + 5,760 + 4,800] = [20,160] Therefore, Total amount received by selling all the books = Rs 20,160
solution_html:
Let the number of books as a 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix=  {tex}B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;\;10\;dozen}&{\;\;\;\;8\;dozen}&{\;\;\;\;10\;dozen} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10 \times 12 = 120}&{8 \times 12 = 96}&{10 \times 12 \times 120} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
Let the selling prices of each book as a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 matrix {tex}S = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80} \\ {60} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total amount received by selling all books = {tex}BS = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {120}&{96}&{120} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {80} \\ {60} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
= [120(80) + 96(60) + 120(40)] = [9,600 + 5,760 + 4,800] = [20,160]
Therefore, Total amount received by selling all the books = Rs 20,160

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Assume X, Y, Z, W, and P are matrices of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n, 3 {tex}\times{/tex} k, 2 {tex}\times{/tex} p, n {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and p {tex}\times{/tex} k, respectively. The restriction on n, k and p so that PY + WY will be defined are
question_html:
Assume X, Y, Z, W, and P are matrices of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n, 3 {tex}\times{/tex} k, 2 {tex}\times{/tex} p, n {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and p {tex}\times{/tex} k, respectively.
The restriction on n, k and p so that PY + WY will be defined are

options.explanation: Matrices P and Y are of the orders p {tex}\times{/tex} k and 3 {tex}\times{/tex} k respectively. Therefore, matrix PY will be defined if k = 3. Then, PY will be of the order p {tex}\times{/tex} k = p {tex}\times{/tex} 3. Matrices W and Y are of the orders n {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and 3 {tex}\times{/tex} k = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 respectively. As, the number of columns in W is equal to the number of rows in Y, Matrix WY is well defined and is of the order n {tex}\times{/tex} 3. Matrices PY and WY can be added only when their orders are the same. Therefore, PY is of the order p {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and WY is of the order n {tex}\times{/tex} 3. Thus, we must have p = n. Therefore, k = 3 and p = n are the restrictions on n, k and p so that PY + WY will be defined.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Assume X, Y, Z, W, and P are matrices of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n, 3 {tex}\times{/tex} k, 2 {tex}\times{/tex} p, n {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and p {tex}\times{/tex} k, respectively. If n = p, then the order of the matrix 7X – 5Z is
question_html:
Assume X, Y, Z, W, and P are matrices of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n, 3 {tex}\times{/tex} k, 2 {tex}\times{/tex} p, n {tex}\times{/tex} 3 and p {tex}\times{/tex} k, respectively.
If n = p, then the order of the matrix 7X – 5Z is

options.explanation: Matrix X is of the order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n. Therefore, matrix 7X is also of the same order. Matrix Z is of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}= 2\times n ~~~~~...(\because p=n){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Matrix 5Z is also of the same order. Now, both the matrices 7X and 5Z are of the order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n. Thus, matrix 7X – 5Z is well- defined and is of the order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} n.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the transpose of the matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {5} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the transpose of the matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {5} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We know that transpose of a matrix is obtained by interchanging the elements of the rows and columns. In other words, we can say, if {tex}\mathrm{A}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]_{m \times \mathrm{n}} \text { then } \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ji}}\right]_{\mathrm{n\times m}}{/tex} So, let {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {5} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {-1} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex} Therefore, transpose of the given matrix A is denoted by A’ Hence, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} The transpose of the given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that transpose of a matrix is obtained by interchanging the elements of the rows and columns. In other words, we can say, if
{tex}\mathrm{A}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]_{m \times \mathrm{n}} \text { then } \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ji}}\right]_{\mathrm{n\times m}}{/tex}
So, let {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {5} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} \\ {-1} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex}
Therefore, transpose of the given matrix A is denoted by A’
Hence, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
The transpose of the given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the transpose of the matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the transpose of the matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We know that transpose of a matrix is obtained by interchanging the elements of the rows and columns. In other words, we can say, if {tex}\mathrm{A}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]_{m \times \mathrm{n}} \text { then } \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ji}}\right]_{\mathrm{n\times m}}{/tex} So, let {tex}B ={/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, transpose of the given matrix B denoted by B’ is given by {tex} B' {/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So,Transpose of the given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that transpose of a matrix is obtained by interchanging the elements of the rows and columns. In other words, we can say, if
{tex}\mathrm{A}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]_{m \times \mathrm{n}} \text { then } \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ji}}\right]_{\mathrm{n\times m}}{/tex}
So, let   {tex}B ={/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, transpose of the given matrix B denoted by B’ is given by
             {tex}  B' {/tex}= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So,Transpose of the given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the transpose of the matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {6} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {5} & {6} \\ {2} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the transpose of the matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {6} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {5} & {6} \\ {2} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We known that transpose of a matrix is obtained by interchanging the elements of the rows and columns. In other words, we can say, if {tex}{A}=\left[{a}_{{i} j}\right]_{m\times n }{/tex} then {tex}{A}^{\prime}=\left[{a}_{\mathrm{ji}}\right]_{\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{m}}{/tex} So, let {tex}\mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {6} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {5} & {6} \\ {2} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, the transpose of the given matrix C is denoted by C' and given by {tex}C^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {5} & {5} & {3} \\ {6} & {6} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} i.e, Transpose of the given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {5} & {5} & {3} \\ {6} & {6} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We known that transpose of a matrix is obtained by interchanging the elements of the rows and columns. In other words, we can say, if {tex}{A}=\left[{a}_{{i} j}\right]_{m\times n }{/tex} then {tex}{A}^{\prime}=\left[{a}_{\mathrm{ji}}\right]_{\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{m}}{/tex} 
So, let {tex}\mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {6} \\ {\sqrt{3}} & {5} & {6} \\ {2} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
Therefore, the transpose of the given matrix C is denoted by C' and given by
{tex}C^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {5} & {5} & {3} \\ {6} & {6} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
i.e,  Transpose of the given matrix is {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {\sqrt{3}} & {2} \\ {5} & {5} & {3} \\ {6} & {6} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then verify (A + B)′ = A′ + B′,
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then verify (A + B)′ = A′ + B′,
solution_text: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} (A+B)’ = A’+B’ Explanation: We will first calculate L.H.S i.e. (A+B)’ and then consecutively we will calculate R.H.S and verify that both are equal. So, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1+(-4)} & {2+1} & {3+(-5)} \\ {5+1} & {7+2} & {9+0} \\ {-2+1} & {1+3} & {1+1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {3} & {-2} \\ {6} & {9} & {9} \\ {-1} & {4} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}(A+B)^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {6} & {-1} \\ {3} & {9} & {4} \\ {-2} & {9} & {2} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(1) Noe, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So, A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}+\mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1+(-4)} & {5+1} & {-2+1} \\ {2+1} & {7+2} & {1+3} \\ {3+(-5)} & {9+0} & {1+1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {6} & {-1} \\ {3} & {9} & {4} \\ {-2} & {9} & {2} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) &( 2), we see that (A+B)’ = A’+B’. Hence verified.
solution_html:
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(A+B)’ = A’+B’
Explanation: We will first calculate L.H.S i.e. (A+B)’ and then consecutively we will calculate R.H.S and verify that both are equal.
So, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1+(-4)} & {2+1} & {3+(-5)} \\ {5+1} & {7+2} & {9+0} \\ {-2+1} & {1+3} & {1+1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {3} & {-2} \\ {6} & {9} & {9} \\ {-1} & {4} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}(A+B)^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {6} & {-1} \\ {3} & {9} & {4} \\ {-2} & {9} & {2} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(1)
Noe, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}+\mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1+(-4)} & {5+1} & {-2+1} \\ {2+1} & {7+2} & {1+3} \\ {3+(-5)} & {9+0} & {1+1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {6} & {-1} \\ {3} & {9} & {4} \\ {-2} & {9} & {2} \end{array}\right] {/tex}  ...(2)
From equation (1) &( 2), we see that
(A+B)’ = A’+B’. Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then verify (A – B)′ = A′ – B′
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then verify (A – B)′ = A′ – B′
solution_text: We will first calculate L.H.S i.e. (A+B)’ and then consecutively we will calculate R.H.S and verify that both are equal. So, A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1-(-4)} & {2-1} & {3-(-5)} \\ {5-1} & {7-2} & {9-0} \\ {-2-1} & {1-3} & {1-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {1} & {8} \\ {4} & {5} & {9} \\ {-3} & {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}(A-B)^\prime{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {4} & {-3} \\ {1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {8} & {9} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(1) Now, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So, A' - B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1-(-4)} & {5-1} & {-2-1} \\ {2-1} & {7-2} & {1-3} \\ {3-(-5)} & {9-0} & {1-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}-\mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {4} & {-3} \\ {1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {8} & {9} & {0} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) & (2) we verify that {tex}(A-B)^\prime=A^\prime-B^\prime{/tex} . Hence verified.
solution_html:
We will first calculate L.H.S i.e. (A+B)’ and then consecutively we will calculate R.H.S and verify that both are equal.
So, A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7} & {9} \\ {-2} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1-(-4)} & {2-1} & {3-(-5)} \\ {5-1} & {7-2} & {9-0} \\ {-2-1} & {1-3} & {1-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {1} & {8} \\ {4} & {5} & {9} \\ {-3} & {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}(A-B)^\prime{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {4} & {-3} \\ {1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {8} & {9} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(1)
Now, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, A' - B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {2} & {7} & {1} \\ {3} & {9} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-4} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1-(-4)} & {5-1} & {-2-1} \\ {2-1} & {7-2} & {1-3} \\ {3-(-5)} & {9-0} & {1-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}-\mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {4} & {-3} \\ {1} & {5} & {-2} \\ {8} & {9} & {0} \end{array}\right] {/tex}...(2)
From equation (1) & (2) we verify that
{tex}(A-B)^\prime=A^\prime-B^\prime{/tex}.
Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify (A + B)′ = A′ + B′
question_html: If {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify (A + B)′ = A′ + B′
solution_text: Given, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3+(-1)} & {-1+2} & {0+1} \\ {4+1} & {2+2} & {1+3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {1} & {1} \\ {5} & {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (A+B)' =\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {5} \\ {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}(A+B)^\prime{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {5} \\ {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(1) Now, A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {2} \\ {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {5} \\ {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) & (2) we verify that (A+B)’ = A’+B’. Hence verified.
solution_html:
Given,  {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3+(-1)} & {-1+2} & {0+1} \\ {4+1} & {2+2} & {1+3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {1} & {1} \\ {5} & {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (A+B)' =\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {5} \\ {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}(A+B)^\prime{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {5} \\ {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}...(1)
Now, A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {2} \\ {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {5} \\ {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}...(2)
From equation (1) & (2) we verify that
(A+B)’ = A’+B’. Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify (A – B)′ = A′ – B′
question_html: If {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify (A – B)′ = A′ – B′
solution_text: Given, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3-(-1)} & {-1-2} & {0-1} \\ {4-1} & {2-2} & {1-3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {4} & {-3} & {-1} \\ {3} & {0} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}(A-B)^\prime{/tex} = {tex} \left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \\ {-1} & {-2} \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}~~~~~~A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {2} \\ {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}-\mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \\ {-1} & {-2} \end{array}\right] {/tex} From equation (1) & (2) we see that (A - B)’ = A’ - B’. Hence verified.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {0} \\ {4} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {1} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3-(-1)} & {-1-2} & {0-1} \\ {4-1} & {2-2} & {1-3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {4} & {-3} & {-1} \\ {3} & {0} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}(A-B)^\prime{/tex} = {tex} \left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \\ {-1} & {-2} \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex}~~~~~~A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {4} \\ {-1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {1} \\ {2} & {2} \\ {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}-\mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \\ {-1} & {-2} \end{array}\right] {/tex}
From equation (1) & (2) we see that
(A - B)’ = A’ - B’. Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathbf{B}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then find (A + 2B)′
question_html: If A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathbf{B}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then find (A + 2B)′
solution_text: Here, {tex}A^\prime{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {1} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So, {tex}A+2 B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {-2} & {1} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]+2\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+2 B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {1} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {0} \\ {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+2 B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {-4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, (A+2B)' = transpose of A+2B {tex}\Rightarrow(A+2 B)^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-4} & {5} \\ {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, 
{tex}A^\prime{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {1} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, {tex}A+2 B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {-2} & {1} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]+2\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-1} & {0} \\ {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+2 B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {1} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {0} \\ {2} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+2 B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} {-4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, (A+2B)' = transpose of A+2B
{tex}\Rightarrow(A+2 B)^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-4} & {5} \\ {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For the matrices, A and B, verify that (AB)′ = B′A′, where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {-4} \\ {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: For the matrices, A and B, verify that (AB)′ = B′A′, where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {-4} \\ {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {-4} \\ {3} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Explanation: The product of two matrices is defined or possible only if the number of columns of the former matrix is equal to a number of rows of the latter matrix. {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {-4} \\ {3} \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1 \times(-1)} & {1 \times 2} & {1 \times 1} \\ {-4 \times(-1)} & {-4 \times 2} & {-4 \times 1} \\ {3 \times(-1)} & {3 \times 2} & {3 \times 1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {4} & {-8} & {-4} \\ {-3} & {6} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So, {tex}(\mathrm{AB})^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {4} & {-3} \\ {2} & {-8} & {6} \\ {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right] ~~~~~~...(i){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{c} {-1} \\ {2} \\ {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{c} {-1} \\ {2} \\ {1} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1 \times 1} & {-1 \times(-4)} & {-1 \times 3} \\ {2 \times 1} & {2 \times(-4)} & {2 \times 3} \\ {1 \times 1} & {1 \times(-4)} & {1 \times 3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {4} & {-3} \\ {2} & {-8} & {6} \\ {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right] ....(ii){/tex} From equation (i) & (ii) we see that (AB)’ = B’A’. Hence verified.
solution_html:
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {-4} \\ {3} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Explanation: The product of two matrices is defined or possible only if the number of columns of the former matrix is equal to a number of rows of the latter matrix.
{tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {-4} \\ {3} \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1 \times(-1)} & {1 \times 2} & {1 \times 1} \\ {-4 \times(-1)} & {-4 \times 2} & {-4 \times 1} \\ {3 \times(-1)} & {3 \times 2} & {3 \times 1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {4} & {-8} & {-4} \\ {-3} & {6} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, {tex}(\mathrm{AB})^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {4} & {-3} \\ {2} & {-8} & {6} \\ {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right] ~~~~~~...(i){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{c} {-1} \\ {2} \\ {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{c} {-1} \\ {2} \\ {1} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1 \times 1} & {-1 \times(-4)} & {-1 \times 3} \\ {2 \times 1} & {2 \times(-4)} & {2 \times 3} \\ {1 \times 1} & {1 \times(-4)} & {1 \times 3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-1} & {4} & {-3} \\ {2} & {-8} & {6} \\ {1} & {-4} & {3} \end{array}\right] ....(ii){/tex}
From equation (i) & (ii) we see that
(AB)’ = B’A’. Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For the matrices, A and B, verify that (AB)′ = B′A′, where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0} \\ {1} \\ {2} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: For the matrices, A and B, verify that (AB)′ = B′A′, where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0} \\ {1} \\ {2} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Given that, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0} \\ {1} \\ {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Explanation: The product of two matrices is defined or possible only if the number of columns of the former matrix is equal to number of rows of the latter matrix. {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0} \\ {1} \\ {2} \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0 \times 1} & {0 \times 5} & {0 \times 7} \\ {1 \times 1} & {1 \times 5} & {1 \times 7} \\ {2 \times 1} & {2 \times 5} & {2 \times 7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} & {7} \\ {2} & {10} & {14} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}(\mathrm{AB})^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {2} \\ {0} & {5} & {10} \\ {0} & {7} & {14} \end{array}\right] ~~~~~~...(1){/tex} Now, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {1} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {1} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1 \times 0} & {1 \times 1} & {1 \times 2} \\ {5 \times 0} & {5 \times 1} & {5 \times 2} \\ {7 \times 0} & {7 \times 1} & {7 \times 2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {2} \\ {0} & {5} & {10} \\ {0} & {7} & {14} \end{array}\right] ~~~~~~~...(2){/tex} From equation (1) & (2) we see that (AB)' = B'A'. Hence verified.
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0} \\ {1} \\ {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Explanation: The product of two matrices is defined or possible only if the number of columns of the former matrix is equal to number of rows of the latter matrix.
{tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0} \\ {1} \\ {2} \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0 \times 1} & {0 \times 5} & {0 \times 7} \\ {1 \times 1} & {1 \times 5} & {1 \times 7} \\ {2 \times 1} & {2 \times 5} & {2 \times 7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} & {7} \\ {2} & {10} & {14} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}(\mathrm{AB})^\prime=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {2} \\ {0} & {5} & {10} \\ {0} & {7} & {14} \end{array}\right] ~~~~~~...(1){/tex}
