Now, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right] \text { and } B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {1} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {1} \\ {5} \\ {7} \end{array}\right] \left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1 \times 0} & {1 \times 1} & {1 \times 2} \\ {5 \times 0} & {5 \times 1} & {5 \times 2} \\ {7 \times 0} & {7 \times 1} & {7 \times 2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {2} \\ {0} & {5} & {10} \\ {0} & {7} & {14} \end{array}\right] ~~~~~~~...(2){/tex}
From equation (1) & (2) we see that
(AB)' = B'A'. Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then verify that A′ A = I
question_html: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then verify that A′ A = I
solution_text: We know A’ can be calculated by taking the transpose of the given matrix A. Therefore, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now multiply A and A’. So, {tex}A A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {(\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha)+(\sin \alpha) \times(\sin \alpha)} & {\cos \alpha \times(-\sin \alpha)+(\sin \alpha) \times \cos \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha \times \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \times(\sin \alpha)} & {-\sin \alpha \times(-\sin \alpha)+\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} & {\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] ...(1) \quad\left(\because \cos \alpha^{2}+\sin \alpha^{2}=1\right){/tex} And we know ‘I’ represents an identity matrix Therefore, {tex}I=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(2) From equation 1 & 2 we can say that AA’ = I AA’ = I. Hence verified.
solution_html:
We know A’ can be calculated by taking the transpose of the given matrix A.
Therefore, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now multiply A and A’. So,
{tex}A A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {(\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha)+(\sin \alpha) \times(\sin \alpha)} & {\cos \alpha \times(-\sin \alpha)+(\sin \alpha) \times \cos \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha \times \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \times(\sin \alpha)} & {-\sin \alpha \times(-\sin \alpha)+\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} & {\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] ...(1) \quad\left(\because \cos \alpha^{2}+\sin \alpha^{2}=1\right){/tex}
And we know ‘I’ represents an identity matrix
Therefore, {tex}I=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] {/tex}...(2)
From equation 1 & 2 we can say that
AA’ = I
AA’ = I. Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then verify that A′ A = I
question_html: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then verify that A′ A = I
solution_text: Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha} \\ {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha} \\ {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~ \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\sin \alpha \times \sin \alpha+\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha \times(-\cos \alpha)+\cos \alpha \times \sin \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha \times \sin \alpha+\sin \alpha \times \cos \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha \times(-\cos \alpha)+\sin \alpha \times \sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha \sin \alpha+\sin \alpha \cos \alpha} & {\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] = I \quad\left(\because \cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha=1\right){/tex} Therefore, AA' = I Hence verified.
solution_html:
Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha} \\ {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}A A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha} \\ {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow~ \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\sin \alpha \times \sin \alpha+\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha \times(-\cos \alpha)+\cos \alpha \times \sin \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha \times \sin \alpha+\sin \alpha \times \cos \alpha} & {-\cos \alpha \times(-\cos \alpha)+\sin \alpha \times \sin \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha+\cos \alpha \sin \alpha} \\ {-\cos \alpha \sin \alpha+\sin \alpha \cos \alpha} & {\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{AA}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] = I \quad\left(\because \cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha=1\right){/tex}
Therefore, AA' = I
Hence verified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {5} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} is a symmetric matrix.
question_html: Show that the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {-1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {5} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}is a symmetric matrix.
solution_text: A matrix is said to be symmetric only if the transpose of a matrix and the matrix itself are equal or the same. This means that A = A'. Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {5} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(1) Therefore, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {5} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(2) Therefore,from equation (1) & (2) we obtain A = A', Therefore, we can say that matrix A is a Symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
A matrix is said to be symmetric only if the transpose of a matrix and the matrix itself are equal or the same. This means that A = A'. Here, 
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {5} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(1)
Therefore, 
{tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} & {1} \\ {5} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(2)
Therefore,from equation (1) & (2) we obtain
A = A',
Therefore, we can say that matrix A is a Symmetric matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {0} & {1} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} is a skew-symmetric matrix.
question_html: Show that the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {0} & {1} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}is a skew-symmetric matrix.
solution_text: A matrix is said to be skew-symmetric if the transpose of the matrix is equal to the negative of the matrix. This means that A' = -A. Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(1) Therefore,we get {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(2) Now if we carefully look at the equation 2 we can rewrite it as {tex}A^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} (by taking - 1 common) Therefore, A' = (-1) {tex}\times{/tex} A (from equation 1) ⇒ A' = -A, Therefore, we can say that matrix A is a Skew-symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
A matrix is said to be skew-symmetric if the transpose of the matrix is equal to the negative of the matrix. This means that A' = -A. Here,
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(1)
Therefore,we get
{tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ...(2)
Now if we carefully look at the equation 2 we can rewrite it as
{tex}A^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {1} & {-1} \\ {-1} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}(by taking - 1 common)
Therefore, A' = (-1) {tex}\times{/tex} A (from equation 1)
⇒ A' = -A,
Therefore, we can say that matrix A is a Skew-symmetric matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , verify that (A + A′) is a symmetric matrix.
question_html: For the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, verify that (A + A′) is a symmetric matrix.
solution_text: Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} On adding them we get, {tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+1} & {5+6} \\ {6+5} & {7+7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {11} \\ {11} & {14} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(1) Now, {tex}(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime})^\prime=A^\prime+A={/tex} {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {11} \\ {11} & {14} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(2) So, from equation (1) & (2) we get, (A + A') = (A + A')', hence we can say that (A + A') is a Symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
Here,
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
On adding them we get,
{tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+1} & {5+6} \\ {6+5} & {7+7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {11} \\ {11} & {14} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(1)
Now, {tex}(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime})^\prime=A^\prime+A={/tex} {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {11} \\ {11} & {14} \end{array}\right] {/tex} ...(2)
So, from equation (1) & (2) we get,
(A + A') = (A + A')', hence we can say that (A + A') is a Symmetric matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , verify that (A – A′) is a skew-symmetric matrix
question_html: For the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, verify that (A – A′) is a skew-symmetric matrix
solution_text: Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right] \text { and } A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} subtracting A' from A, we get, {tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1-1} & {5-6} \\ {6-5} & {7-7} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {0} & {-1} \\ {1} & ~~~{0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Clearly, {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {1} \\ {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]~~~...(i){/tex} We can rewrite the above equation as {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)^\prime=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-1} \\ {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]=(-1)(A-A^\prime) ~~~~~... using (i){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A – A')' = -(A – A') Hence we can say that matrix A is a Skew-symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
Here, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right] \text { and } A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
subtracting A' from A, we get,
{tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {5} \\ {6} & {7} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {6} \\ {5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1-1} & {5-6} \\ {6-5} & {7-7} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {0} & {-1} \\ {1} & ~~~{0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Clearly, {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {1} \\ {-1} & {0} \end{array}\right]~~~...(i){/tex}
We can rewrite the above equation as
{tex}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)^\prime=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-1} \\ {1} & {0} \end{array}\right]=(-1)(A-A^\prime) ~~~~~... using (i){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (A – A')' = -(A – A')
Hence we can say that matrix A is a Skew-symmetric matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + A’) and {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A – A’) when {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + A’) and {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A – A’) when {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - b} \\ a&0&{ - c} \\ b&c&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}A + A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - b} \\ a&0&{ - c} \\ b&c&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 0}&{a - a}&{b - b} \\ { - a + a}&{0 + 0}&{c - c} \\ { - b + b}&{ - c + c}&{0 + 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}(A + A') = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}A - A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - b} \\ a&0&{ - c} \\ b&c&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 - 0}&{a + a}&{b + b} \\ { - a - a}&{0 - 0}&{c + c} \\ { - b - b}&{ - c - c}&{0 - 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2a}&{2b} \\ { - 2a}&0&{2c} \\ { - 2b}&{ - 2c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}(A - A') = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2a}&{2b} \\ { - 2a}&0&{2c} \\ { - 2b}&{ - 2c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - b} \\ a&0&{ - c} \\ b&c&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}A + A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - b} \\ a&0&{ - c} \\ b&c&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 0}&{a - a}&{b - b} \\ { - a + a}&{0 + 0}&{c - c} \\ { - b + b}&{ - c + c}&{0 + 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}(A + A') = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}A - A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - b} \\ a&0&{ - c} \\ b&c&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 - 0}&{a + a}&{b + b} \\ { - a - a}&{0 - 0}&{c + c} \\ { - b - b}&{ - c - c}&{0 - 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2a}&{2b} \\ { - 2a}&0&{2c} \\ { - 2b}&{ - 2c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}(A - A') = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2a}&{2b} \\ { - 2a}&0&{2c} \\ { - 2b}&{ - 2c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&b \\ { - a}&0&c \\ { - b}&{ - c}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Express matrix as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Express matrix as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix:{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this, we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.” Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {1} \\ {5} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, on adding A and A’ we will get, {tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {1} \\ {5} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3+3} & {5+1} \\ {1+5} & {-1+(-1)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {6} \\ {6} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}M=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {6} \\ {6} & {-2} \end{array}\right] = \left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}M^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M' = M Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M. Now on subtracting A’ from A we will get, {tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {1} \\ {5} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}N=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {2} \\ {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, N' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-2} \\ {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {2} \\ {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N' = - N Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a skew-symmetric matrix as N' = -N Now, Add M and N, we get, {tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {2} \\ {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3+0} & {3+2} \\ {3+(-2)} & {-1+0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So we see here, M + N = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N. Hence proved.
solution_html:
As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this, we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.”
Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {1} \\ {5} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, on adding A and A’ we will get,
{tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {1} \\ {5} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3+3} & {5+1} \\ {1+5} & {-1+(-1)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {6} \\ {6} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, {tex}M=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {6} \\ {6} & {-2} \end{array}\right] = \left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}M^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M' = M Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M.
Now on subtracting A’ from A we will get,
{tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {1} \\ {5} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, {tex}N=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-4} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {2} \\ {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, N' ={tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-2} \\ {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {2} \\ {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N' = - N
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a skew-symmetric matrix as N' = -N
Now, Add M and N, we get,
{tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {3} \\ {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {2} \\ {-2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3+0} & {3+2} \\ {3+(-2)} & {-1+0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So we see here, M + N = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {5} \\ {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A
Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.
Hence proved.

question_text: Express matrix as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Express matrix as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this, we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.” Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, on adding A and A’ we will get, {tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {6+6} & {-2+(-2)} & {2+2} \\ {-2+(-2)} & {3+3} & {-1+(-1)} \\ {2+2} & {-1+(-1)} & {3+3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {12} & {-4} & {4} \\ {-4} & {6} & {-2} \\ {4} & {-2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Let, M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {12} & {-4} & {4} \\ {-4} & {6} & {-2} \\ {4} & {-2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow M=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}M^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M' = M Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M Now, on subtracting A’ from A we will get, {tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6-6} & {-2-(-2)} & {2-2} \\ {-2} & {3-3} & {-1-(-1)} \\ {2-2} & {-1-(-1)} & {3-3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}N^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N’ = - N Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as N' = - N. Now, Add M and N, we get, {tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6+0} & {-2+0} & {2+0} \\ {-2+0} & {3+0} & {-1+0} \\ {2+0} & {-1+0} & {3+0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So we see her, M + N = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.
solution_html:
As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this, we will be using Theorem  which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.”
Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, on adding A and A’ we will get,
{tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {6+6} & {-2+(-2)} & {2+2} \\ {-2+(-2)} & {3+3} & {-1+(-1)} \\ {2+2} & {-1+(-1)} & {3+3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {12} & {-4} & {4} \\ {-4} & {6} & {-2} \\ {4} & {-2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Let, M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {12} & {-4} & {4} \\ {-4} & {6} & {-2} \\ {4} & {-2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow M=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}M^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M' = M
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M
Now, on subtracting A’ from A we will get,
{tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6-6} & {-2-(-2)} & {2-2} \\ {-2} & {3-3} & {-1-(-1)} \\ {2-2} & {-1-(-1)} & {3-3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}N^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N’ = - N
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as N' = - N.
Now, Add M and N, we get,
{tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6+0} & {-2+0} & {2+0} \\ {-2+0} & {3+0} & {-1+0} \\ {2+0} & {-1+0} & {3+0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So we see her, M + N = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-2} & {3} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A

Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.

solution_text: As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this, we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.” Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-2} & {-4} \\ {3} & {-2} & {-5} \\ {-1} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, on adding A and A’ we will get, {tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-2} & {-4} \\ {3} & {-2} & {-5} \\ {-1} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3+3} & {3+(-2)} & {-1+(-4)} \\ {-2+3} & {-2+(-2)} & {1+(-5)} \\ {-4+(-1)} & {-5+1} & {2+2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {6} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {-4} & {-4} \\ {-5} & {-4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {-4} & {-4} \\ {-5} & {-4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathrm{M}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M' = M Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M Now, on subtracting A’ from A we will get, {tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-2} & {-4} \\ {3} & {-2} & {-5} \\ {-1} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3-3} & {3-(-2)} & {-1-(-4)} \\ {-2-3} & {-2-(-2)} & {1-(-5)} \\ {-4-(-1)} & {-5-1} & {2-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {5} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {6} \\ {-3} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {5} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {6} \\ {-3} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {\frac{5}{2}} & {\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {0} & {3} \\ {\frac{-3}{2}} & {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathrm{N}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {\frac{-5}{2}} & {\frac{-3}{2}} \\ {\frac{5}{2}} & {0} & {-3} \\ {\frac{3}{2}} & {3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=-\mathrm{N}{/tex} Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as N' = - N w, Add M and N, we get, {tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {\frac{5}{2}} & {\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {0} & {3} \\ {\frac{-3}{2}} & {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3+0} & {\frac{1}{2}+\frac{5}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}+\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}+\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2+0} & {-2+3} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}+\frac{-3}{2}} & {-2+(-3)} & {0+2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{6}{2}} & {\frac{-2}{2}} \\ {\frac{-4}{2}} & {-2} & {1} \\ {\frac{-8}{2}} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So we see here, M + N = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex} Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.
solution_html:
As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this, we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.”
Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

Therefore, A' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-2} & {-4} \\ {3} & {-2} & {-5} \\ {-1} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, on adding A and A’ we will get,
{tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-2} & {-4} \\ {3} & {-2} & {-5} \\ {-1} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3+3} & {3+(-2)} & {-1+(-4)} \\ {-2+3} & {-2+(-2)} & {1+(-5)} \\ {-4+(-1)} & {-5+1} & {2+2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {6} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {-4} & {-4} \\ {-5} & {-4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {1} & {-5} \\ {1} & {-4} & {-4} \\ {-5} & {-4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\mathrm{M}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M' = M
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M
Now, on subtracting A’ from A we will get,
{tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-2} & {-4} \\ {3} & {-2} & {-5} \\ {-1} & {1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3-3} & {3-(-2)} & {-1-(-4)} \\ {-2-3} & {-2-(-2)} & {1-(-5)} \\ {-4-(-1)} & {-5-1} & {2-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {5} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {6} \\ {-3} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {5} & {3} \\ {-5} & {0} & {6} \\ {-3} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {\frac{5}{2}} & {\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {0} & {3} \\ {\frac{-3}{2}} & {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\mathrm{N}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {\frac{-5}{2}} & {\frac{-3}{2}} \\ {\frac{5}{2}} & {0} & {-3} \\ {\frac{3}{2}} & {3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=-\mathrm{N}{/tex}
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as N' = - N
w, Add M and N, we get,
{tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{1}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {\frac{5}{2}} & {\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}} & {0} & {3} \\ {\frac{-3}{2}} & {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3+0} & {\frac{1}{2}+\frac{5}{2}} & {\frac{-5}{2}+\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}+\frac{-5}{2}} & {-2+0} & {-2+3} \\ {\frac{-5}{2}+\frac{-3}{2}} & {-2+(-3)} & {0+2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {\frac{6}{2}} & {\frac{-2}{2}} \\ {\frac{-4}{2}} & {-2} & {1} \\ {\frac{-8}{2}} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So we see here, M + N = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {3} & {-1} \\ {-2} & {-2} & {1} \\ {-4} & {-5} & {2} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex}

Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.

question_text: Express matrix as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Express matrix as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Given A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Explanation: As per Theorem “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.” Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, on adding A and A’ we will get, {tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+1} & {5+(-1)} \\ {-1+5} & {2+2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, M' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{\prime}=\mathrm{M}{/tex} Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M Now, on subtracting A’ from A we will get, {tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-1} & {5-(-1)} \\ {-1-5} & {2-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {6} \\ {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} Therefore, N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {6} \\ {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow N=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, N' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-3} \\ {3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=-\mathrm{N}{/tex} Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a skew-symmetric matrix as N' = - N Now, Add M and N, we get, {tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+0} & {2+3} \\ {2+(-3)} & {2+0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So we see here, {tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex} Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.
solution_html:
Given A ={tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Explanation: As per Theorem  “Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.” So in order to prove this we will be using Theorem which states that “For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + A’ is a symmetric matrix and A – A’ is a skew-symmetric matrix.”
Now, Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, on adding A and A’ we will get,
{tex}A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+1} & {5+(-1)} \\ {-1+5} & {2+2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A+A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {4} \\ {4} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, M' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{\prime}=\mathrm{M}{/tex}
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a symmetric matrix as M' = M
Now, on subtracting A’ from A we will get,
{tex}A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {5} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-1} & {5-(-1)} \\ {-1-5} & {2-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A-A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {6} \\ {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, Let N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex}
Therefore, N = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {6} \\ {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow N=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, N' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {-3} \\ {3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{\prime}=-\mathrm{N}{/tex}
Thus M = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right){/tex} is a skew-symmetric matrix as N' = - N
Now, Add M and N, we get,
{tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {2} & {2} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {3} \\ {-3} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1+0} & {2+3} \\ {2+(-3)} & {2+0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\mathrm{N}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So we see here, {tex}M+N=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {5} \\ {-1} & {2} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex}
Thus, A is represented as the sum of a symmetric matrix M and a skew-symmetric matrix N.

question_text: If A, B are symmetric matrices of same order, then AB – BA is a
question_html: If A, B are symmetric matrices of same order, then AB – BA is a

options.explanation: Given A and B are symmetric matrix of same order {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = A' .......(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = B' ......(ii) So, AB - BA = A'B' - B'A' ...(from eqn (i) and (ii) ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - BA = (BA)' - (AB)' ... ( {tex}\because{/tex} (AB)' = B'A') {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - BA = (-1) ((AB)' - (BA)') ...(taking -1 common) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - BA = -(AB - BA)' ...( {tex}\because{/tex} (A - B)' = A' - B') Here we see that the relation between (AB - BA) and its transpose i.e. (AB - BA)' is (AB - BA) = - (AB -BA)', this implies that (AB - BA) is a skew symmetric matrix.

question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , and A + A' = I, if the value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, and A + A' = I, if the value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is

options.explanation: Given {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also given that A + A' = I ...(1) (Putting the values in equation (1)) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \alpha+\cos \alpha} & {-\sin \alpha+\sin \alpha} \\ {\sin \alpha-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha+\cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2 \cos \alpha} & {0} \\ {0} & {2 \cos \alpha} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know the two matrices are equal only when all their corresponding elements or entries are equal i.e. if A = B, then a ij and b ij for all i and j. This implies, {tex}2 \cos \alpha=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}~~~ ...\left(\because \cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

question_html: Matrices A and B will be inverse of each other only if

options.explanation: Here it is given that A & B are inverse of each other. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A -1 = B ...(i) Also B -1 = A ...(ii) From definition of inverse matrix, we know that- AA -1 = I {tex}\therefore{/tex} A -1 = B ...from eq (i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} AB = AA^{-1}{/tex} = I...(iii) Similarly, BB -1 = I {tex}\therefore{/tex} B -1 = A ...(from eq(ii)) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BA = BB −1 = I ...(iv) So, from (iii) and (iv), we get AB = BA = I

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A and B are symmetric matrices, prove that AB – BA is a skew symmetric matrix.
question_html: If A and B are symmetric matrices, prove that AB – BA is a skew symmetric matrix.
solution_text: A and B are symmetric matrices. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A’ = A and B’ = B ……….(i) Now, (AB - BA)’ = (AB)’ - (BA)’ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB - BA)’ = B’A’ - A’B’ [Reversal law] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB - BA)’ = BA - AB [Using eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB - BA)’ = - (AB - BA) Therefore, (AB - BA) is a skew symmetric.
solution_html:
A and B are symmetric matrices. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A’ = A and B’ = B ……….(i)
Now, (AB - BA)’ = (AB)’ - (BA)’ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB - BA)’ = B’A’ - A’B’ [Reversal law]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB - BA)’ = BA - AB [Using eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB - BA)’ = - (AB - BA)
Therefore, (AB - BA) is a skew symmetric.

question_text: Show that the matrix B’AB is symmetric or skew symmetric according as A is symmetric or skew symmetric.
question_html: Show that the matrix B’AB is symmetric or skew symmetric according as A is symmetric or skew symmetric.
solution_text: (B'AB)' = [B'(AB)]' = (AB)' (B')' [ {tex}\because{/tex} (CD)' = D'C'] ​ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ​ (B'AB)' = B'A'B ……….(i) Case I: A is a symmetric matrix, ​ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ​ A' = A {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) (B'AB)' = B'AB {tex}\therefore{/tex} B'AB is a symmetric matrix. Case II: A is a skew symmetric matrix. ​ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ​ A' = - A Putting A' = - A in eq. (i), (B'AB)' = B'(- A)B = - B'AB {tex}\therefore{/tex} B'AB is a skew symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
(B'AB)' = [B'(AB)]' = (AB)' (B')' [{tex}\because{/tex} (CD)' = D'C']
​{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}​ (B'AB)' = B'A'B ……….(i)
Case I: A is a symmetric matrix,
​{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}​ A' = A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) (B'AB)' = B'AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} B'AB is a symmetric matrix.
Case II: A is a skew symmetric matrix.
​{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}​ A' = - A
Putting A' = - A in eq. (i),
(B'AB)' = B'(- A)B = - B'AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} B'AB is a skew symmetric matrix.

question_text: Find the values of x, y, z if the matrix {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} satisfy the equation A'A = I.
question_html: Find the values of x, y, z if the matrix {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} satisfy the equation A'A = I.
solution_text: It is given that: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} From the given question, A'.A = I {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + {x^2} + {x^2}}&{0 + xy - xy}&{0 - xy + xz} \\ {0 + xy - xy}&{4{y^2} + {y^2} + {y^2}}&{2yz - yz - yz} \\ {0 - zx + zx}&{2yz - yz - yz}&{{z^2} + {z^2} + {z^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2}}&0&0 \\ 0&{6{y^2}}&0 \\ 0&0&{3{z^2}} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating corresponding entries,we obtain 2x 2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} And 6y 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow {y^2} = \frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex} And 3z 2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\;Z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
From the given question, A'.A = I {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + {x^2} + {x^2}}&{0 + xy - xy}&{0 - xy + xz} \\ {0 + xy - xy}&{4{y^2} + {y^2} + {y^2}}&{2yz - yz - yz} \\ {0 - zx + zx}&{2yz - yz - yz}&{{z^2} + {z^2} + {z^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2}}&0&0 \\ 0&{6{y^2}}&0 \\ 0&0&{3{z^2}} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries,we obtain
2x2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
And 6y2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow {y^2} = \frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex}
And 3z2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\;Z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For what values of x: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {0} \\ {2} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0.
question_html: For what values of x: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {0} \\ {2} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0.
solution_text: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {0} \\ {2} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1+4+1} & {2+0+0} & {0+2+2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{6} & {2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [6(0) + 2(2) + 4(x)] = O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [4 + 4x] = [0] {tex}\therefore{/tex} 4 + 4x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1. Therefore,the required value of x is-1.
solution_html:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1} & {2} & {0} \\ {2} & {0} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = O
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{1+4+1} & {2+0+0} & {0+2+2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = O
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}{6} & {2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l}{0} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = O
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [6(0) + 2(2) + 4(x)] = O
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [4 + 4x] = [0]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4 + 4x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1.

Therefore,the required value of x is-1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} show that A 2 - 5A + 7I = 0.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} show that A2 - 5A + 7I = 0.
solution_text: Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 – 5A + 7I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 1}&{3 + 2} \\ { - 3 - 2}&{ - 1 + 4} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&5 \\ { - 5}&3 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 - 15}&{5 - 5} \\ { - 5 + 5}&{3 - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&0 \\ 0&{ - 7} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7 + 7}&{0 + 7} \\ {0 + 7}&{ - 7 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} L.H.S= R.H.S. Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 – 5A + 7I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 1}&{3 + 2} \\ { - 3 - 2}&{ - 1 + 4} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&5 \\ { - 5}&3 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 - 15}&{5 - 5} \\ { - 5 + 5}&{3 - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&0 \\ 0&{ - 7} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7 + 7}&{0 + 7} \\ {0 + 7}&{ - 7 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} L.H.S= R.H.S. Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
question_html: Find x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 0 - 2}&{0 - 10 - 0}&{2x - 5 - 3} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 2}&{ - 10}&{2x - 8} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [(x - 2)x - 10(4) + (2x - 8)1] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 - 2x - 40 + 2x - 8] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {{x^2} - 48} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = {\left[ 0 \right]_{1 \times 1}}{/tex} Equating corresponding entries, we have x 2 - 48 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 48 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4\sqrt 3{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{ - 5}&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 0 - 2}&{0 - 10 - 0}&{2x - 5 - 3} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {x - 2}&{ - 10}&{2x - 8} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [(x - 2)x - 10(4) + (2x - 8)1] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x2 - 2x - 40 + 2x - 8] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {{x^2} - 48} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = {\left[ 0 \right]_{1 \times 1}}{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries, we have
x2 - 48 = 0
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 48
 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \pm 4\sqrt 3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A manufacturer produces three products, x, y, z which he sells in two markets. Annual sales are indicated below: Market Products I 10000 2,000 18,000 II 6000 20,000 8,000 If unit sale prices of x, y and z are Rs 2.50, Rs 1.50 and Rs 1.00 respectively, find the total revenue in each market with the help of matrix algebra. If the unit costs of the above three commodities are Rs 2.00, Rs 1.00 and 50 paise respectively. Find the gross profit.
question_html:
A manufacturer produces three products, x, y, z which he sells in two markets. Annual sales are indicated below:

Market Products
I 10000 2,000 18,000
II 6000 20,000 8,000
  1. If unit sale prices of x, y and z are Rs 2.50, Rs 1.50 and Rs 1.00 respectively, find the total revenue in each market with the help of matrix algebra.
  2. If the unit costs of the above three commodities are Rs 2.00, Rs 1.00 and 50 paise respectively. Find the gross profit.
solution_text: According to question, the matrix {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\mathop {10,000}\limits^x }&{\mathop {2,000}\limits^y }&{\mathop {18,000}\limits^z } \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6,000}&{20,000}&{8,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} Let B be the column matrix representing sale price of each unit of products x, y, z. Then, {tex}B = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2.5} \\ {1.5} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}{/tex} Now Revenue = Sale price * Number of items sold {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10,000}&{2,000}&{18,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6,000}&{20,000}&{8,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2.5} \\ {1.5} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25,000 + 3,000 + 18,000} \\ {15,000 + 30,000 + 8,000} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {46,000} \\ {53,000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, the revenue collected by sale of all items in Market I = Rsb 46,000 and the revenue collected by sale of all items in Market II = Rs 53,000. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z. Then {tex}C = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ {0.5} \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total cost = AC = {tex}\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10,000}&{2,000}&{18,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6,000}&{20,000}&{8,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ {0.5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {20,000 + 2,000 + 9,000} \\ {12,000 + 20,000 + 4,000} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {31,000} \\ {36,000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The profit collected in two markets is given in matrix form as Profit matrix = Revenue matrix – Cost matrix {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {46,000} \\ {53,000} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {31,000} \\ {36,000} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15,000} \\ {17,000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, the gross profit in both the markets = Rs 15000 + Rs 17000 = Rs 32,000
solution_html:
According to question, the matrix {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\mathop {10,000}\limits^x }&{\mathop {2,000}\limits^y }&{\mathop {18,000}\limits^z } \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6,000}&{20,000}&{8,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}

  1. Let B be the column matrix representing sale price of each unit of products x, y, z.
    Then, {tex}B = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2.5} \\ {1.5} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}{/tex}
    Now Revenue = Sale price * Number of items sold
    {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10,000}&{2,000}&{18,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6,000}&{20,000}&{8,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2.5} \\ {1.5} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25,000 + 3,000 + 18,000} \\ {15,000 + 30,000 + 8,000} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {46,000} \\ {53,000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
    Therefore, the revenue collected by sale of all items in Market I = Rsb 46,000 and the revenue collected by sale of all items in Market II = Rs 53,000.
  2. Let C be the column matrix representing cost price of each unit of products x, y, z.
    Then {tex}C = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ {0.5} \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total cost = AC = {tex}\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10,000}&{2,000}&{18,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6,000}&{20,000}&{8,000} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \\ {0.5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
    {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {20,000 + 2,000 + 9,000} \\ {12,000 + 20,000 + 4,000} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {31,000} \\ {36,000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}The profit collected in two markets is given in matrix form as
    Profit matrix = Revenue matrix – Cost matrix
    {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {46,000} \\ {53,000} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {31,000} \\ {36,000} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15,000} \\ {17,000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
    Therefore, the gross profit in both the markets = Rs 15000 + Rs 17000 = Rs 32,000

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the matrix X so that {tex}X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
question_html: Find the matrix X so that {tex}X\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4b} \\ {c + 4d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5b} \\ {2c + 5d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a + 6b} \\ {3c + 6d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} On solving a + 4b = -7 and 2a + 5b = -8 & c + 4d = 2 and 2c + 5d = 4 we get a = 1, b = -2, c = 2, d = 0 {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4b} \\ {c + 4d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 5b} \\ {2c + 5d} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a + 6b} \\ {3c + 6d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7} \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ 4 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 9} \\ 6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
On solving a + 4b = -7 and  2a + 5b = -8 & c + 4d = 2 and 2c + 5d = 4
we get a = 1, b = -2, c = 2, d = 0
{tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2} \\ 2&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha} & {\beta} \\ {\gamma} & {-\alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} is such that A 2 = I, then
question_html: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha} & {\beta} \\ {\gamma} & {-\alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} is such that A2 = I, then

options.explanation: In the given question: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\alpha} & {\beta} \\ {\gamma} & {-\alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Calculating A 2 : A 2 = A.A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha} & {\beta} \\ {\gamma} & {-\alpha} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha} & {\beta} \\ {\gamma} & {-\alpha} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha \cdot \alpha+\beta \cdot \gamma} & {\alpha \cdot \beta+\beta \cdot(-\alpha)} \\ {\gamma \cdot \alpha+(-\alpha) \cdot \gamma} & {\gamma \cdot \beta+(-\alpha) \cdot(-\alpha)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha^{2}+\beta \gamma} & {\alpha \cdot \beta-\alpha \cdot \beta} \\ {\gamma \cdot \alpha-\gamma \cdot \alpha} & {\gamma \cdot \beta+\alpha^{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha^{2}+\beta \gamma} & {0} \\ {0} & {\beta \gamma+\alpha^{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} And given that A 2 = I Therefore, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\alpha^{2}+\beta \gamma} & {0} \\ {0} & {\beta \gamma+\alpha^{2}} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Comparing corresponding elements we obtain {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha^{2}+\beta \gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\alpha^{2}-\beta \gamma=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If the matrix A is both symmetric and skew symmetric, then
question_html: If the matrix A is both symmetric and skew symmetric, then

options.explanation: Only a null matrix can be symmetric as well as skew symmetric. In Symmetric Matrix A T = A, Skew Symmetric Matrix A T = -A, Given that the matrix is satisfying both the properties. Therefore, Equating the RHS we get A = -A i.e, 2A = 0. Therefore A = 0, which is a null matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If A is square matrix such that A 2 = A, then (I + A) 3 – 7 A is equal to
question_html: If A is square matrix such that A2 = A, then (I + A)3 – 7 A is equal to

options.explanation: Given that A 2 = A Calculating value of (I + A) 3 - 7A: {tex}(I+A)^3 - 7A=I^{3}+A^{3}+3 I^{2} A+3 I A^{2}-7 A{/tex} {tex}= \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{A}^{2}-7 \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{I}^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I} \text { and } \mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}\right){/tex} {tex}= I+A \cdot A+3 A+3 A-7 A\left(A^{2}=A\right){/tex} {tex}= I+A^{2}+3 A+3 A-7 A{/tex} = {tex}I +7A-7A{/tex} Hence, (I + A) 3 - 7A = {tex}I{/tex}
